entries.title: ResearchOS Wiki
headings: First time here? Read this first.
Sharing a folder with your lab?
bodySnippets: Everything you need to set up ResearchOS, share a folder with your lab, and use each feature. Most users only need the first two sections, Getting Started and Shared Lab Accounts , to be productive.

ResearchOS is a single-page research-management app that reads and writes directly to a folder on your computer. There's no server, no account, no sign-up. Your data lives in plain JSON files you fully own.
Open ResearchOS in Chrome, Edge, or Brave . Other browsers can't read local folders yet.

Click Link Folder to pick an existing folder on your disk, or Create New Folder to start fresh. This is your ResearchOS folder. If you want a quick intro to the app first, BeakerBot waves at you from the upper-right of this screen with an optional 3-minute walkthrough button. ...
Pick a username. Your data lives under inside that folder. You can set an optional password to gate access on a shared machine.

Once you sign in, BeakerBot's in-product welcome tour auto-starts on the empty account. (This is the longer hands-on tour, separate from the optional 3-minute walkthrough on the folder picker.) Click through to learn the major surfaces. See Welcome Wizard for a breakdown of ea...
Start adding projects, methods, experiments, and Gantt tasks. Everything syncs to disk immediately.
The ? icon in the top-right of the app header always opens this wiki.
On the folder-connect screen, look for the New here? Read the setup guide link, which goes to the Connecting Your Folder page.
Use Ctrl + F / Cmd + F to search within a wiki page.

Looking for security and privacy details? The Security page covers data storage, encryption, and what never leaves your machine. It does not appear in the section grid above because it has no sub-pages.
The wiki is big because the app is big. If you only have time for one page, Start Here covers the 7 things worth knowing up front and how to navigate the rest of the wiki when you actually need it.

Read Shared Lab Accounts first. Putting the ResearchOS folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud lets multiple lab members use the same folder, but you have to configure it to stay downloaded locally . If you don't, ResearchOS won't be able to read and write rel...

entries.title: Compliance
bodySnippets: Plain-English answers to the two questions labs ask before they trust an electronic notebook with grant-funded data: does ResearchOS support the NIH Data Management & Sharing Policy, and how does it compare to LabArchives?

entries.title: ResearchOS vs LabArchives
bodySnippets: An honest side-by-side. LabArchives is the incumbent most academic labs are leaving, so it is the right thing to measure against. ResearchOS wins decisively on data ownership and cost; LabArchives is still ahead on a few specific things, and this page says so plainly.

LabArchives is a hosted, cloud ELN sold per seat. You trade money and data custody for managed backups, security certifications, and turnkey institutional administration. It suits labs that want a vendor to hold the data and an IT department that wants a certified cloud contract.

ResearchOS is a free, open-source, local-first ELN. Your data is a folder of open files on your own disk, shared across a lab through whatever cloud drive you already pay for. It suits labs that want to own their data outright, avoid per-seat fees, and keep unpublished work of...
Saying otherwise would not survive a demo, so here it is straight:

You own your data, in open formats, forever. The single biggest difference. Your work is plain files on your disk, not rows in a vendor database. NIH's repository guidance prizes exactly this kind of portability and non-proprietary format.
No per-seat fees. A ten-person lab on LabArchives Professional is several thousand dollars a year, every year. ResearchOS is free.
Unpublished research never leaves your control. No vendor server holds your pre-publication data. For sensitive or competitive work, that is a real advantage, not a checkbox.
Built for bench work. The visual PCR editor, plate layouts, LC gradients, cell-culture schedules, Gantt scheduling, and lab purchasing are first-class, not bolt-ons.
Switching in is easy. ResearchOS imports your LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP directly, so you are not retyping years of notebooks.
One-click sharing endpoint. Built-in Figshare export and DOI minting. In ResearchOS you export and deposit into a repository yourself.
Complete per-entry revision history. Shipped today in LabArchives; still on the ResearchOS roadmap.
Security certifications. FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, and 21 CFR Part 11. If your institution mandates a certified cloud vendor, this is decisive, and ResearchOS does not compete here by design.
Managed backups and any-browser access. The vendor handles redundancy, and there is no File System Access API browser requirement.

If your priorities are owning your data in open formats, eliminating per-seat cost, and keeping unpublished work off a vendor server, ResearchOS is the better choice and supports your NIH DMS compliance fully (see NIH Data Management & Sharing ). If your institution requires a...

entries.title: NIH Data Management & Sharing
headings: There is no such thing as a certified ELN
What the policy actually asks for
Where your data actually gets shared
So what are universities actually paying for?
NIH does not want your lab notebook
No built-in repository or DOI integration
Per-entry edit history is on the roadmap
Structured grant and ORCID fields are in
bodySnippets: Vendors advertise "NIH Data Management & Sharing support" like it is a certificate they bought. It is not. This page explains what the policy actually requires, why no electronic notebook is "NIH certified," and exactly how ResearchOS supports your compliance.

The NIH Data Management and Sharing (DMS) Policy, in effect since January 25 2023, binds researchers and institutions that receive NIH funding. It does not bind software. There is no NIH program that audits, licenses, or certifies an electronic lab notebook, and there is no ba...

Compliance is something a researcher does, not something a tool is. You comply by writing a Data Management and Sharing Plan with your grant application and then following it. The notebook is where the work that the plan describes actually happens. Any ELN with the right featu...
NIH expects funded investigators to do three things:

ResearchOS supports all three expectations. The same plan / create / implement structure other ELN vendors use to map their features to the policy applies here, with the bonus that your data lives in open formats on your own disk the entire time.

Use separate projects to model how each grant's data will be organized, and reusable methods and templates so the whole lab captures data the same way from day one. Planning the structure up front is most of what a DMS Plan's data-organization section asks you to describe.

The plan itself is a short document built against NIH's required format. Most institutions point researchers at the free DMPTool , which has the official NIH template built in. Draft it there, then keep the finished plan in ResearchOS as a note or attachment alongside the proj...

This is where the day-to-day work lives, and where ResearchOS is strongest. The table below maps what the policy and NIH's Desirable Characteristics of Data Repositories guidance expect against the ResearchOS feature that delivers it.

The DMS Plan names a repository, and that repository is where sharing happens, not the notebook. NIH does not mandate a specific one. It publishes Desirable Characteristics of Data Repositories (persistent identifiers, long-term stewardship, curation, clear reuse terms) and ex...

ResearchOS gets you to that handoff cleanly. You export the curated experiment or dataset, then upload it to the repository your plan names. The repository is what mints the DOI or other persistent identifier and hosts the public copy. Because your ResearchOS data is already i...

ResearchOS supports your DMS compliance, and it is honest about where a feature is a manual step rather than a built-in one. None of these block compliance, but you should know them before you write your plan.
Do not anchor a compliance decision to a vendor blog (including this one). The primary sources are short and readable:
Wondering how ResearchOS stacks up against the tool most labs are leaving? See ResearchOS vs LabArchives .
Plan and budget for managing and sharing scientific data, before the work starts.
Write a DMS Plan (a two-page document) and submit it with the funding application.
Implement the plan : manage the data rigorously during the project, then share it no later than publication or the end of the award, whichever comes first.
NIH DMS Policy overview and the policy hub at sharing.nih.gov .
NOT-OD-21-013 : the Final NIH Policy itself.
NOT-OD-21-014 : the required elements of a DMS Plan. This is the real checklist.
NOT-OD-21-016 : desirable characteristics of data repositories.

Not a DMS certificate. The enterprise price tag buys security certifications (FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, 21 CFR Part 11), hosted backups, institutional administration, and brand trust. The "NIH selected LabArchives" line you may have seen refers to NIH's intramural program (NI...

This is the part most vendor pages bury. NIH explicitly says investigators are not expected to share laboratory notebooks, preliminary analyses, drafts, or communications. What you share is a curated dataset , deposited into an appropriate repository. So a notebook's real job ...

ResearchOS does not push directly to Zenodo or Figshare and does not mint DOIs itself. You export, then deposit into the repository yourself, and the repository issues the identifier. This is one extra manual step compared to LabArchives' Figshare and DOI buttons.

The append-only PI audit log and soft-delete Trash exist today. A full per-record revision history with a revert button is planned, not shipped. If your plan or institution requires a complete versioned history of every entry, account for that gap now.

ResearchOS now has first-class fields for your ORCID iD (on your profile) and for funder and award numbers (on each funding account), named to match the DataCite schema a DOI deposit uses. The remaining piece is the one-click deposit that reads them automatically; until that l...

ResearchOS holds no FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, or 21 CFR Part 11 attestation. Its security posture is different by design: a local-first app with no vendor database to certify (see Security ). That is the right trade-off for many labs, but if your institution requires a certif...

entries.title: Features
bodySnippets: One short page per feature page in ResearchOS. Each has a screenshot, a few steps for the most common action, and any non-obvious tips.

entries.title: Calendar
headings: A tab has to be open
bodySnippets: A month, week, and day calendar for your conferences, deadlines, and meetings, with optional overlays from Google, Outlook, and iCloud.

The Calendar page lays out your ResearchOS events on a grid alongside any read-only items pulled in from a linked Google, Outlook, or iCloud calendar. Both kinds of event render with a filled colored background; the text color flips between black and white automatically so the...

The header strip holds the current date heading, prev/next arrows, a Today shortcut, and a month / week / day toggle. The two buttons on the far right are Linked Calendars (connect Google, Outlook, or paste an iCal URL) and + New Event .

Click a ResearchOS event block to open its details popover. Edit swaps the popover into a form with every field, including a "Clear times (make all-day)" shortcut. Save writes the change. Delete removes the event after a confirmation.

Click Linked Calendars in the top right (or Manage in the sidebar) to open the modal. You can also navigate to directly, which auto-opens the modal so you can share a deep-link that drops someone straight into the add-feed flow. The same iCal-URL flow works for all four provid...

Every linked calendar gets a color in the sidebar legend. The Linked Calendars modal also has a per-feed color picker so you can recolor any feed to match your preferences without disconnecting and re-adding it. Toggle a feed on or off from either the modal or the legend. Remo...

Clicking a linked event opens a read-only popover with the title, time, location, and any notes the source calendar published. To edit a linked event, open it in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar) and the change shows up in ResearchOS on the next 15-minu...
Want the deep-dive? Calendar Feeds walks through per-provider setup with screenshots.

Reminders are a single global preference, set from the Reminders link at the top of the calendar sidebar. The modal lets you turn reminders on, pick how far ahead you want to be notified (anywhere from 1 minute to 1 day before), and optionally grant browser-notification permis...

When a timed event's reminder fires, ResearchOS adds an entry to the bell inbox in the top bar. If you've granted browser permission, it also raises a system notification you can click to jump back to the tab. All-day events don't produce reminders, since there's no obvious mo...
On the Calendar page the usual left sidebar swaps in a calendar-specific one with two sections:

Month is a 7-column grid of day cells. Each cell shows up to three events, and the rest collapse into a count (a non-clickable label, not a link). Single-click a day to open a day-detail drawer listing every event on that date. Double-click to start a new event prefilled with ...

Week is a 7-day stack with an all-day strip across the top and an hourly time grid below. Click any empty 15-minute slot in the grid to open the New Event popover prefilled with that date and time. A red "now" line tracks the current minute on today's column.
Day shows the same hourly grid for a single day, also with a red "now" line tracking the current minute. Useful first thing in the morning, and roomy enough to show an event's location inline.
Google Calendar exposes a per-calendar Secret address in iCal format under Settings → Settings for my calendars. Copy that URL, paste it into the form.
Outlook / Microsoft 365 publishes a calendar from Settings → Calendar → Shared calendars → Publish a calendar. Copy the ICS link Outlook generates, paste it into the form.
iCloud / Apple Calendar shares via Calendar app → right-click → Share Calendar → Public Calendar. Paste the URL as-is. ResearchOS rewrites it automatically.
Other (any iCal URL) accepts any standard feed URL, so you can add lab-managed or third-party calendars that aren't one of the three named providers.

Calendars lists ResearchOS events plus every linked feed, color-matched to the grid. Click a feed row to hide or show its events without disconnecting it. The Reminders and Manage links sit at the top of this section.
Upcoming shows the next 30 days, grouped by date (Today / Tomorrow / weekday + date) with up to 40 entries. Click any item to jump the main calendar to that day in day view.

Click + New Event in the top right. You can also double-click a day in month view, or single-click an empty slot in week or day view, to open the popover prefilled with that date (and a 15-minute snapped start time when applicable).
Fill in a Title , pick a Type (Conference, Deadline, Meeting, or Other), and set the start and end dates. Leaving both time fields blank makes it an all-day event.
Optionally add a Location , a URL , a Color from the palette, and free-form Notes .
Click Create Event . The block appears in the selected date(s) in whichever view is active.

The month/week/day toggle changes your view for the current session only. The change is ephemeral: when you reload the app, the calendar returns to its persisted startup view. To change that startup view, open Settings → View defaults and pick a different starting view. Resear...
If you don't pick a color, the event uses its type's default: purple for conferences, red for deadlines, blue for meetings, gray for other.

The event create/edit modal includes a Mark as PTO day checkbox. Events flagged PTO display a badge on their chip in the calendar grid. ResearchOS also factors PTO days into streaks and project schedule calculations, so marking time off keeps your progress tracking accurate.
A public iCal URL grants read access to every event on that calendar to anyone who has the link. ResearchOS only stores it in your private data folder, but treat the URL itself like a password.

Reminders run in JavaScript inside an open ResearchOS tab. If every tab is closed at the moment the reminder is due, the real-time popup won't fire, but the reminder is still waiting in the bell inbox the next time you open the app.
The Calendar page in month view, with native event blocks and the +New Event button in the top right.
The Linked Calendars modal showing the Connected list at top and an iCal URL form below.

entries.title: The Workbench
headings: Three tabs, three ways to slice the work
Opening an experiment: the four-tab popup
Attach a method (or a PCR protocol)
Variation Notes and per-run PCR tweaks
Notes intentionally skips the project filter
When to use Notes vs Lab Notes
Lists holds list tasks, not experiments
Pick the format by where the file is going
Exporting more than one at a time
Sub-tasks live on list tasks, not experiments
bodySnippets: The Workbench is where active research lives day to day. One page, three tabs (Experiments, Notes, Lists), each with its own way of grouping work. The URL is /workbench, and /experiments still redirects there for backwards compatibility.

The Workbench packs three different views of your in-flight work into one page. The tab strip across the top names them and gives each a color identity: Experiments (blue), Notes (emerald), Lists (violet). The active tab fills with its color; the other two read as gray text bu...

Each tab uses a different organizing principle, which is the whole point of keeping them apart. The Experiments tab groups protocols by where they sit in the run cycle (ready, blocked, running, awaiting writeup, recent results, archive). The Lists tab groups list tasks by how ...

Directly below the tab strip sits a row of colored pills, one per project, plus an All projects pill on the left. Click a pill to narrow the view to that project; click it again (or pick a different one) to clear. The filter applies across tabs: pick a project on Experiments a...

Some tasks never get filed under a project: a one-off experiment you spin up in the "Miscellaneous" standalone slot stays project-less on purpose. To move a task into or out of a project later, open it and use the project dropdown on the popup's Details tab. Alongside your rea...
The Experiments tab organizes every experiment task in your library by stage of the protocol. Six sections stack vertically, always in this order:

Experiments scheduled to begin today or recently, with no incomplete upstream task blocking the start. These are the cards you can pick up and run today, so the section sits at the top and reads first.

Experiments that depend on an upstream task that hasn't finished yet. The blocking parent's name renders directly under the card in a small amber strip so you know what you're waiting on. Click the parent name to jump straight to that task and unblock the chain.

Experiments where today falls between the start date and the end date. Each card carries a Day N of M freshness label, and (if the experiment is part of a chain) a gray Next: pointer to the experiment that follows it so you can step through a multi-day protocol without leaving...

Experiments marked complete but with no results body or attached images on disk yet. This section is your reminder pile: bench work is done, the writeup is still owed. The header stays put even when the section is empty, with a bordered emerald chip reading "All recent experim...

Completed experiments with results landed in the last 30 days. When you have results across two or more projects, this section sub-groups them by project (a small colored dot per project header) so a busy month doesn't collapse into one undifferentiated grid.

The archive: completed experiments older than 30 days. The section header carries a Flat / By project toggle so you can flip between a single time-ordered grid and one block per project. There's no time cap, so the whole completion history is reachable from one place.

Each card carries the experiment name, a project pill, the project color along the edge, a hero image (if the writeup has one), a method chip strip, and a freshness label keyed to the section it's in. When two or more experiments are linked by a dependency, they collapse into ...
The blue button at the top right of the tab opens the New Task modal with the type pre-selected to experiment . It is the same modal you'd get from the Gantt.

The Notes tab holds every note that isn't tied to a specific experiment. Meeting notes from a Tuesday lab meeting, a running log of equipment-room weirdness, half-formed ideas you want to find again next month: they all land here, in one flat list with no section headers. The ...

The header carries a search box on the left and three type filter buttons in the middle ( All , Single , Running Logs ). Single notes are one-page records of a moment (a meeting, an idea). Running logs are timestamped multi-entry pages you append to over time (an equipment jou...

The Lists tab is the queue view of every list task you own. Lists are the non-experiment task type: checklists, paperwork, ordering tasks, errands, anything that isn't a protocol. The Lists tab sorts them into five stages by how their dates relate to today, stacked top-to-bott...

Each card carries the task name, a project color dot with the project name, and a date signal phrased relative to today ("3d overdue", "Started yesterday", "Starts in 4d"). Clicking a card expands it inline as an accordion panel. Opening one card collapses the previously-open ...
Clicking a list card expands it in place. A violet border highlights the open card, and a panel slides down below the header row. Inside the panel:

Clicking any experiment card opens a centered modal. The popup always opens on the Details tab, regardless of whether the experiment is ready, running, awaiting writeup, or completed. A consistent entry point matters more than a smart guess at where you're heading next: you la...
The popup carries four tabs across the header:

The header row also holds a green completion checkmark, an Export icon (down arrow, experiments only), a Share button, a fullscreen toggle, and Delete. Drag any file onto the popup and it lands in the last-viewed editor tab's or folder, with a confirmation toast at the drop po...

The two markdown tabs hold different kinds of writing on the same experiment. Lab Notes is the during-the-run record: running observations, what reagents you grabbed, what went sideways, the photo you took at the bench five minutes ago. Results is the final-output writeup: the...

Both tabs use the same editor surface: a markdown body with a three-way Edit / Hybrid / Preview mode toggle, an image strip pinned below the editor, and a 📎 Add File toolbar button. The full editor reference (shortcuts, block-level editing in Hybrid, the language picker, imag...

Drag images straight into the body to place them inline, or drop them on the strip to attach without inlining. The Files sub-tab holds non-image attachments (PDFs especially), and renders them as a thumbnail grid you can open in a new tab.

Each attached method has a collapsible amber Variation Notes panel at the top of its tab. Click + Add Note and the editor prepends a fresh heading so each entry is timestamped. Hover any rendered entry to reveal an in-place delete button. The notes save back to the experiment,...

For PCR-typed methods, the gradient editor and recipe table below the variation panel are editable per-experiment. Reset to Method drops your overrides back to the protocol's defaults, and Save Changes writes them to this task only.

Click the down-arrow icon in the popup header (tooltip: Export experiment ) to open the Export dialog. The dialog heading reads Export <experiment name> and lists three format cards, each with a short description of what you get and who it's for. Click a card and the file down...

While the file builds, the dialog shows a Preparing export… spinner and disables every button (Escape and the backdrop are also locked) so you can't kick off a second export by accident. If anything goes wrong, an alert shows the error and the dialog stays open for a retry.
Overdue : list tasks whose end date has passed and that aren't complete yet. The first thing in your queue every morning.
Doing : list tasks where today falls between start and end.

Upcoming : list tasks scheduled to start within the next 14 days. Tasks starting further out are omitted from the main list; a small gray footnote below the section reads "+ N scheduled later than 14d out" so you know they exist without cluttering the view.
Recently done : completed in the last 30 days.
Earlier : completed more than 30 days ago. Collapsed by default; click the header to expand.
Name row : click the name to rename it inline. Press Enter or click away to save.
Sub-task checklist : tick individual items to check them off. Click an item's text to rename it. The delete icon appears on hover to remove an item.
Add item : type in the input and press Enter (or click Add ) to append a new item to the checklist.
Mark list complete button in the footer: marks the whole list done and cascades all un-checked items to complete. Click again to mark it incomplete.
Open full view link in the footer: opens the legacy full-screen popup for the same task, useful for the Details and date-editing fields not exposed in the inline panel.
Details is the entry tab. It holds the name, dates, duration, project, tags, and the high-level toggles. If the task carries a (legacy) deviation log string, it renders here in an amber box.
Lab Notes is the during-the-run markdown body plus a Files sub-tab for PDFs and other non-image attachments.
Method shows one tab per attached method, with the Variation Notes panel and (for PCR methods) an editable gradient and recipe.
Results is the second markdown editor, for the final writeup (the gel image you'd put in a paper, the plot for a thesis chapter), with its own Files sub-tab.

PDF report : a professional, printable PDF with a title page, a clickable table of contents, an outline pane (bookmarks) for jumping between sections, and inline images. Lab Notes, Results, every attached method (rendered, with per-run PCR tweaks), Sub-tasks, the Deviation log...

HTML report : a single self-contained page bundled in a with its attachments. Images are base64-inlined so the page renders even offline; PDF methods and other non-image files sit alongside in , , and . Open it in any browser, or mail the zip to a collaborator who doesn't run ...

Raw ResearchOS format : the full experiment as a sharable bundle ( , , the raw and , each method as its own JSON file including PCR protocols, plus every attached image and file in the same folder layout the app uses on disk). This is the format another ResearchOS user imports...
Open the Method tab and click the + button on the tab strip.

The Method Picker opens with Recently used in this project and Recently used pinned at the top, the full library below grouped by folder, and a search box that also takes queries. PCR protocols show up alongside markdown methods (their tab gets a small PCR badge).
Pick a method. It becomes a new tab on the strip. Attach as many as the experiment needs.

Notes aren't tied to any single project, so the pill strip hides on this tab by design. A filter that can't actually narrow anything would just be visual noise. To scope the note grid, use the search box at the top of the tab and filter by content instead.

The Notes tab is for writing that isn't attached to any single experiment. Per-experiment lab notes (running observations, what reagents you grabbed, the photo at the bench five minutes ago) live inside that experiment's popup on its Lab Notes tab. If the writing belongs to a ...

Experiment tasks always render on the Experiments tab, never on Lists, even when their dates would make them overdue or upcoming. The split is by task type, not by status. The Lists tab is for the to-do work that supports your protocols, not the protocols themselves.
Lab Notes saves to and Results saves to . Attachments split per-tab into , , , and . PDFs added through the Files sub-tab land in or .

PDF for thesis chapters, IRB filings, lab-meeting handouts, and anything destined for print. HTML for sharing a polished read-only report with a collaborator who works outside ResearchOS. Raw for archival, lab handoff, or when another ResearchOS user is going to import the exp...

The Export icon on an experiment popup is single-experiment only. To bundle a batch of experiments into one download, use Select on the Search page. Tick the result cards you want, click Export selected , and the same dialog packages every chosen experiment into an with one en...

The sub-task checklist you may have seen on the Home page belongs to the list task type, not experiment . Experiment popups don't carry a sub-task list. If you want a checklist alongside an experiment, make a separate list task and link it as a dependency.
The Workbench in its default state: Experiments tab selected, sections stacked top-to-bottom.
The Workbench page with the Experiments tab active, showing the three-tab strip, the project filter pills, and the stacked section headers.
Each section reads as an uppercase header with a count, plus a one-line helper on the right summarizing the rule (e.g., 'Today falls between start and end date').
The Experiments tab scrolled to show several stacked section headers (Ready to start, Blocked, Running, Awaiting writeup) with cards underneath each.

entries.title: Feedback
headings: Why a pre-filled URL instead of a POST
bodySnippets: The feedback button is a thin layer over GitHub Issues. Click it, pick a type (bug, feature, feedback), fill in the modal, and ResearchOS opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. Nothing submits automatically: you see the body, you edit it, you click Submit. The whole...

Every page in ResearchOS carries a FeedbackButton in the bottom toolbar (or the corresponding floating cluster on dashboards). Click it and the FeedbackModal mounts. The button is always available because every surface in the app is a fair target for feedback.
Pick a type at the top of the modal:

On submit, the modal does not POST to a server. It builds a GitHub issue URL with the title, body, and label pre-filled in the query string, then opens that URL in a new tab. You see the body GitHub is about to create, you can edit any of it, and the issue does not exist until...

The BugStomp scene (a small BeakerBot moment you might hit during onboarding or after a crash recovery) ends with the same feedback flow. Stomping the bug opens the FeedbackModal pre-set to Bug type and prefilled with a hint from the crash context. It is the same modal as the ...

Bug. Something is broken: a crash, a wrong number, a misrendering. The modal auto-attaches the current route, the browser/OS string, and any recent uncaught error details so the report has enough context for a fix.
Feature. A new capability you wish existed. The modal walks a lighter template (what you want, why) and routes to the feature-request label on GitHub.
Feedback. Anything that does not fit the first two: a comment on UX, a typo, a confused-by-naming note. Routes to the feedback label.
Nothing on your machine moves until you intentionally submit.
You can sanitize anything that landed in the auto-attached error details before posting.
The full report shape is determined by in the repo, so the GitHub side renders a templated form rather than a raw markdown body.

The GitHub Issues template at defines the form fields and label routing on the receiving side. The modal sends a type query parameter that GitHub uses to pre-select the matching template. The result is one consistent shape regardless of who clicked Bug, Feature, or Feedback.

We could have POSTed feedback straight to a webhook or a server we run, and the user would not have to see the body before submission. We did not, because the moment a POST endpoint exists, every typed word is a thing that left your machine before you saw the final copy. The p...
The FeedbackModal with Bug selected. The body is editable; nothing leaves your machine until you click through to GitHub.
The FeedbackModal with the Bug radio selected, an editable Title field, an editable description, and an Auto-attached error details section at the bottom.

entries.title: Gantt Chart
headings: Chain two tasks with a dependency
Filter with the multi-select project dropdown
Drop an image on a task
See a whole chain at once: hover-highlight
Standalone tasks have no project to filter by
bodySnippets: The Gantt timeline holds every active task across every project you can see. The view trades calendar precision for visual rhythm: you scan packed rows of colored bars and your eye picks out the busy weeks, the dependencies that cross projects, and the gaps where work is not y...
Every visual element on the Gantt encodes one specific thing. Once the vocabulary clicks, a scroll across the timeline reads almost like sheet music.

The toolbar at the top of the page is where you scope and zoom the chart. The 2026-05 redesign collapsed the old per-project pill row into a single multi-select dropdown so a lab with many projects can keep the chrome compact.

Grab any bar and drag it to a different day. On drop, the task's start date moves to that day and its duration stays the same. Any downstream tasks linked through a dependency recompute their dates to keep their constraints valid: an FS (start-after) child of a moved parent sl...

Most cascades apply silently. The orange "This change will affect N task(s)" modal only opens when an SF (finish-before) chain would shift a task to start in the past. The modal lists every affected task with its old and new dates and a per-task warning line for the past-date ...

A dependency captures the idea that one piece of bench work cannot sensibly begin (or end) until another one reaches a certain point. On the Gantt you express that by dragging one bar and dropping it on top of another. A popup then asks how the two tasks should be linked:

Once you pick a relationship, the dragged task's start date snaps to whatever the dependency requires. Tasks in the same chain share the same colored top stripe, so you can trace a chain across bars. Drag a bar in the chain and downstream tasks shift with it.

Because bars pack into rows by date rather than by chain, the members of one dependency chain can end up scattered across the timeline. Two cues help you read them as a group. The first is always on: each bar in a chain carries small chain-colored dots in its corner, where the...

The second cue is the hover treatment. Move your cursor over any bar that belongs to a multi-member chain and the whole chain lights up: every bar in that chain stays at full strength and picks up a ring in the chain's color, while every bar outside the chain dims back so the ...
The redesigned toolbar replaced the long pill row with a single dropdown. The interaction is:

Not every task belongs to a project. A quick experiment you spin up without filing it under a research question lives in the "Miscellaneous" standalone slot, with no project attached. Those orphan tasks used to vanish the moment you scoped the chart to a specific project, sinc...

The bottom of the Projects dropdown now carries a Standalone row (set off by a dashed divider and marked with a hollow dashed swatch and a small "no project" hint) whose tooltip reads "Toggle visibility of experiments with no project." Tick it to fold your standalone tasks bac...

To move a task into or out of that bucket, open the task and use the project dropdown in its detail popup. Alongside your real projects it lists a Standalone (no project) option; picking it drops the task off every project, and picking a real project files an orphan task back ...

PIs land on the same Gantt UI as every member, but the timeline pulls from a wider pool. The project dropdown on the left of the toolbar now spans every member's projects across the lab, not just the PI's own. One scroll of the chart shows the whole lab's work side by side.

This shifts the role of the page. For a member, the Gantt is a personal schedule. For a PI, it becomes a coordination surface: dependency chains can stretch across users, the project dropdown doubles as a per-member filter (untick a student's projects to focus on a co-PI's tim...

Drag any image file from Finder or the desktop and drop it on a task bar. The image attaches to that task as a new Images-folder entry and a markdown reference is appended to the task's Lab Notes body. This is the fastest path to file a gel image or a microscope snap straight ...

Right-click any day cell in the date grid to open a context menu with a Mark as PTO / Unmark PTO option. Marked days render with sky-blue diagonal stripes and a small dot in the day header so they stand out at a glance. When a cascade computes new dates for chained tasks, PTO ...
PTO marks are per-user (your time off is private), so a labmate looking at the same chart will not see your stripes unless they are viewing through a PI dashboard that aggregates across members.

Goals appear in two places. Above each week's day headers, every active goal that overlaps the week renders as a thin colored bar (longer goals sit lower). Hover one for a tooltip showing the goal name and days remaining (or overdue). Click a bar to open the goal editor.

The Goal sidebar on the right lists active goals as cards. Each card surfaces SMART subgoals as checkboxes so you can tick them off without leaving the chart. Use the + Goal button in the toolbar to add a new high-level goal.

Click a bar to open the task detail popup, where you can edit name, dates, tags, attached methods, notes, results, and subtasks. The popup is the same one used by the Workbench and the calendar, so changes here sync everywhere.

One colored bar per task. Bar color is the project's color. Bar start and length are the task's start date and duration. Tasks pack into rows automatically (the chart is not grouped by project), so non-overlapping tasks share a row to keep the chart dense.

Dependency stripes. A short top stripe in a contrasting color marks tasks in a dependency chain. Every bar in the same chain shares the same stripe color, so you can trace a chain visually across rows. Standalone tasks show a dimmer per-experiment stripe.

Gradient bars for dependent tasks. When a bar is downstream of another (FS, SS, or SF), the fill renders as a subtle gradient instead of a flat color. The gradient direction mirrors the dependency direction, so you can read whether a task waits on something earlier or constrai...
Type icons. A horizontal-lines icon on the right plus a thicker white left edge marks a list task. A dollar-sign icon and a diagonal stripe mark a purchase task.
High-level tasks. Render in muted amber and sit at the top of the packed rows so they read as overlays on the regular schedule.
Completion state. Completed tasks desaturate and go partially transparent with a checkmark on the right edge. A partial fill on the left of a bar shows sub-task completion progress.
Shared badges. A small bracketed initial like on a bar means this task is shared. The bar still uses the project's color, not the owner's.
Edge fade. Bars that start before or end after the visible window fade to black on that edge to signal that they continue offscreen.
PTO stripes. Days you have marked as PTO render with sky-blue diagonal stripes plus a small dot in the day header. Cascades skip these days when computing dependent task dates.

Weekend hash. Saturday and Sunday columns read as light grey background. Tasks belonging to a 5-day project overlay weekend portions with a diagonal hash so you know work does not actually happen on those days. A 7-day project draws solid across weekends.

Projects dropdown (multi-select). Click it to open the project list. Every project starts checked, so the default chart shows everything. Untick a project to hide its bars. Two quick actions at the top: Select all restores the everyone-checked default; Unselect all clears the ...
Tag chips. When any task or project has tags, a row of tag chips appears next to the project dropdown. Toggle a chip to narrow the chart to tasks matching that tag.
Shared toggle. A small share-icon switch that hides or shows tasks others have shared with you.

Zoom segmented control. Eight buttons: , , , , , , , . Shorter zooms keep day-level detail; longer ones span many weeks. All currently shows an 8-week window starting from the current Monday, not a full-history view.
Week navigator on the right. Step the visible window back and forward one week at a time, with a date picker for jumping to a specific Monday and a Today link that snaps back to this week.
+ Task and + Goal buttons at the far right of the toolbar to add a task or a high-level goal without leaving the chart.
Start at same time (SS). Both tasks begin on the same day. On the chart they line up at the same left edge.
Start after (FS). The dragged task starts the day after the target finishes, so the two sit back to back with no overlap.

Finish before (SF). The dragged task finishes the day before the target starts. This is a strict gap: the predecessor clears the calendar entirely before the successor begins, so the two never share a day even when the predecessor is a single-day task. That keeps "finish befor...
Click Projects to expand the list.
The default is all checked . The chart shows every project until you uncheck something.
Select all at the top of the dropdown restores the everyone-checked default (useful after a long Unselect-and-pick session).
Unselect all clears every checkmark in one click, so you can quickly re-tick only the one or two projects you want to focus on.
The dropdown stays open while you click, so you can stage the right filter set without dropdown-bounce.
Dropping on a Saturday or Sunday in a project that has weekends disabled bumps the start date to the next Monday. Toggle the weekend setting from each project's detail popup on the Home page.

Only experiments can be chained. Lists and purchases are deliberately kept out of dependency chains, since linking a shopping order or a checklist into a scheduling chain tends to create constraints nobody wanted. On the Gantt this shows up as a non-interactive signal: drag a ...
The Gantt at a glance: packed rows of bars colored by project, a multi-select project dropdown in the toolbar, weekend hash, and PTO stripes.
The Gantt timeline with packed rows of colored bars, a multi-select project dropdown in the toolbar, PTO stripes on a Saturday cell, and a dependency stripe linking two bars across rows.
Lab head view: the project dropdown spans every member's projects, so one timeline shows the whole lab.
The Gantt as a PI, with the project dropdown open showing projects from every member in the lab.
Clicking a bar opens the full task editor over the Gantt. Same popup as the Workbench and the calendar.
A task detail popup opened over the Gantt chart, with Details, Lab Notes, Method, and Results tabs.

entries.title: Home & Projects
headings: Projects a labmate shared with you
The canvas sits BELOW the project grid
bodySnippets: The Home page is your project hub. Each project gets a card with its color, a progress bar, and a peek at what's coming up next.

Each card carries the project's color bar at the top, its name, and a 7-day badge if weekends are active. Below that you'll see a progress bar (completed / total tasks with a percentage), an Active / Overdue / Upcoming stats row, and a Next Up list of the next five upcoming ta...
Own project cards show a six-dot drag handle on the right. Cards for projects a labmate shared with you do not have a handle and cannot be reordered.

The color you pick follows the project everywhere: the bar on the Gantt , the badge on the Lab Overview widgets, and the overlay on the calendar. Pick distinct colors early so a busy Gantt stays readable.

Clicking a task name inside the Next Up list opens that task's detail popup directly. Clicking anywhere else on the card navigates to that project's dedicated route (see Project Surface for the full walkthrough of Overview, Results, Methods, Goals, and Activity).
Hover any project card to reveal a three-dots kebab button in the top-right corner. The menu contains the following items:

When a labmate shares a project with you, its card slides into the Home grid right next to your own. The card looks the same as any other (the project's color bar, the same progress bar and Next Up list). There's no special badge or owner pill marking it as shared.

The tasks listed inside the project route are the owner's tasks for that project, the same tasks they see on their own Home page. The progress bar and the stats counts on the card all reflect those tasks too.
On a shared project, a few actions change their behavior:
Whether your edits actually go anywhere depends on the permission the owner picked when they shared:

Individual tasks behave the same way. When you open a task that was shared to you (either directly or by belonging to a shared project), the share icon vanishes and the delete button greys out with a tooltip pointing back to the owner. If you have edit permission, edits to the...

Beyond the project grid, Home is also a customizable widget canvas. New accounts start with two default widgets at the top of the canvas: Upcoming tasks on the left and Today's events on the right. From there, you can add, remove, and reorder widgets from the canvas controls t...
Edit opens the EditProjectModal, where you can rename the project, change its color, edit tags, and toggle the 7-day schedule.
Share opens the share dialog so you can grant a labmate access. This item is hidden on projects that were shared to you (only the owner can grant access).

Archive / Unarchive triggers an amber confirmation dialog. Archived projects keep all their tasks but move to the separate Archived Projects section below the active grid, where they appear as muted cards and stop showing in the Gantt and task sidebar. Unarchive from the same ...

Delete is disabled on projects shared to you. Only the original owner can delete the project. Use the kebab Delete item (or the trash-can icon in the project route's top bar) to permanently remove a project you own. This also deletes every task in the project and cannot be und...
The Share kebab item is hidden. Only the original owner can grant access to a project. If you want a third labmate to see it too, ask the owner to share with them as well.
The Delete item is disabled for receivers. Hover it and a tooltip points back to the owner. Only the original owner can destroy the project file.

View-only receivers see greyed-out edit controls. The Edit item and the Archive action both render but stay disabled with a tooltip explaining that only the owner and edit-permission collaborators can change the project.

Edit permission : the edit form, the archive action, and task edits inside the project route all save back to the original owner's copy of the data. Your changes show up on their Home page too (after their next refresh, since each person's app reads its own files on its own sc...
View permission : edit and archive actions are disabled. Treat the project as read-only and use it as a window into the owner's work.
Drag a card to a new spot in the grid. The order is per-user and persists across sessions. Only own project cards have a drag handle; shared-in cards cannot be reordered.

Archive a project from the kebab menu's Archive Project option. Archived projects keep all their tasks but move below the active grid into a separate Archived Projects section, where they read as muted cards. Their tasks stop showing in the Gantt and the task sidebar. Unarchiv...

+ Add widget in the canvas toolbar opens the palette of every available widget (the same catalog the Lab Overview uses). Clicking +Add auto-flips the canvas into edit mode if it was off, then opens the palette. Drag a tile onto the canvas to pin it.

Drag tiles to reorder with edit mode on (toggled via the Edit layout button in the canvas toolbar). Tiles snap to the grid and the layout persists in your settings sidecar at . Home's layout is independent of your Lab Overview layout (if you are a PI), so you customize each su...
Reset in the canvas toolbar wipes your custom layout and restores the two-widget default. It is a sibling button to Edit layout and +Add widget, not a menu item.
Tools launcher in the header opens any Tool popup directly, without pinning. Useful for one-shot looks.

Default widgets : new accounts start with Upcoming tasks (top) and Today's events (next to it). They cover the two most common "what do I do this morning" questions without crowding the canvas. Pin extras like announcements, comments, or lab activity from the catalog whenever ...

New widgets in the catalog do NOT auto-pin to Home. Home is user-curated: if a new widget variant ships, you have to add it via the palette to see it on your canvas. (Lab Overview behaves differently and auto-appends new lab-overview-eligible widgets to a saved layout.) If you...
Click + New Project at the top right of the Home page.
Type a name (e.g. CRISPR Gene Editing Study ) and pick a color from the row of swatches.
Optionally add tags as a comma-separated list (e.g. ). They show up as chips on the card and in search.
Tick 7-day schedule (weekends active) if work on this project spills into Saturday and Sunday. The Gantt respects this setting when it shifts dates around dependencies.
Click Create Project . The card appears in the active grid.

A built-in project called Miscellaneous holds standalone tasks that don't belong to a research project. The kebab menu is hidden entirely for Miscellaneous. You can share it and open its tasks, but the project itself can't be renamed or removed.

Project sharing is the right tool when one labmate wants a specific other labmate to follow along on a specific project. For a single dashboard that rolls up every labmate's projects, tasks, purchases, and announcements at once, the PI opens the Lab Overview at .

The color you pick on Home drives every project bar on the Gantt, every badge across the Lab Overview widgets, and the calendar overlay. Pick distinct colors early so the Gantt stays readable as the project count grows.

Most dashboards put widgets at the top of the page; Home does the opposite. The widget canvas mounts after your active projects, archived projects, and the empty-state hint, so on a fresh account with a few projects you have to scroll down to see it. Once you know it lives at ...
The Home page after creating a few projects.
Home page showing several colored project cards and the New Project button.
Home. Project grid at the top, widget canvas at the bottom with the two defaults (Upcoming tasks and Today's events) pinned by default. Pin and reorder the widgets that match how you start your day.
The Home page showing the project grid at the top with the two default widgets (Upcoming tasks, Today's events) on the widget canvas at the bottom.

entries.title: Import from LabArchives
bodySnippets: The 7-step wizard that turns a LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP into native ResearchOS tasks, plus the bulk-sort screen for cleaning up after.

The mental model for the importer is one rule: each LabArchives page becomes one ResearchOS task . Every entry on that page (text, headings, attachments, embedded images) is collapsed into the task's Lab Notes body. The page's newest entry timestamp becomes the task's start da...
Notebook folders become projects. By default each folder lands as a new project named , but the wizard lets you point folders at projects you already have or leave them unassigned.

You start from Settings → LabArchives → Open import… , which opens the wizard as a modal. The first-run setup screen has the same entry point under Coming from LabArchives? . If you haven't produced an Offline Notebook ZIP yet, hop over to Exporting from LabArchives first.
The wizard runs a fixed sequence of steps. You can back out at any point before the apply phase starts. The step header in the modal shows where you are.

Every notebook folder that contains pages you're importing gets a row in the Map projects step. Pages directly at the notebook root (no folder above them) don't show up as a mapping row, they just inherit the wizard's default of no project unless you assign them later. Rows th...
The three decisions behave like this:

The Start import button stays disabled while any row has a validation error (empty new-project name, no existing project picked). Once the mapping is valid, clicking Start import kicks off the apply phase.

Right after the wizard finishes, the Done step shows an Open bulk-sort button. Clicking it replaces the wizard with a full-screen list of every task the import just created. Tasks are grouped by their assigned project (or under (no project) if unassigned), and each row exposes...

Tick the checkbox on multiple rows and the top of the screen switches to a bulk action bar: Move to a project, Change type to , or Delete N tasks . Edits write through to disk one row at a time, so you can leave the screen partway through and the work already done stays.
Create new project : the wizard creates a new project named and attaches every page under that folder to it. You can edit the name in the row before applying.
Use existing : pick one of your live (non-archived) projects from the dropdown. Pages under that folder are attached to the project you picked.
No project : the pages land as tasks with no project assignment. You can move them to projects later from the bulk-sort screen or by opening each task individually.
A project dropdown to move that task to a different project (or to no project ).
A task type dropdown to flip the task from (the default) to or .
A Delete button for tasks that don't belong anywhere, useful when an old notebook had stray meeting-notes pages mixed in with experiments.

Form-B inline images, by default. Images that LabArchives stores as cloud URLs aren't in the ZIP. The Fetch images step pulls them in when reachable, otherwise the markdown gets a placeholder you can rehydrate later from the per-task banner.
Live LabArchives links between pages. If a LabArchives page links to another page by its LabArchives URL, the link comes across as plain text. ResearchOS doesn't rewrite those into local task links.
Need to produce the ZIP first? See Exporting from LabArchives .
Curious about how the inline-image rehydration paths work? The LabArchives integration page breaks down Form-A vs Form-B and the DevTools-script path.
Imported tasks look and behave the same as native ResearchOS tasks. The Experiments & Notes page covers the task popup, the Lab Notes editor, and how attachments work once they're on disk.
Choose format. The wizard supports the LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP. PDF and Chrome-print formats are on the roadmap and show as Coming soon cards on this step. Pick the ZIP option and continue.

Upload ZIP. Drag the file you downloaded from your LabArchives confirmation email onto the drop zone, or click to pick it from disk. The wizard validates that the file is a and warns if it's larger than 500 MB (the parser holds the archive in browser memory, so multi-gigabyte ...

Preview notebook. The wizard parses the ZIP and shows what it found: folder count, page count, entry count, attachment count, plus a collapsible tree of the notebook structure. If LabArchives left some inline images as URLs rather than bundling them, an amber banner here calls...

Map projects. One row per top-level notebook folder. Each row offers three decisions: Create new project (the default, with a suggested name you can edit), Use existing (pick from your live project list), or No project (the pages on that branch land unassigned). Project mappin...

Fetch images (optional). If the notebook has inline images that LabArchives stores as URLs (and you're not in demo mode), the wizard offers a step to bring those images in. Two paths are available, both credential-free: a generated DevTools script you paste into a browser tab ...

Importing. A progress bar walks through the two write phases: creating new projects first, then writing one task directory per page. Leave the tab open. Each page becomes with a , a folder for attachments, a folder for inline images, and a sidecar (inside the subdirectory, not...

Done. The summary lists how many tasks and projects landed, how many pages were skipped as duplicates of earlier imports, how many online-only images were rehydrated, and any per-page warnings. From here you can close the wizard, or jump straight to Open bulk-sort to re-classi...

ResearchOS reads the ZIP and stops there. It never calls back into LabArchives to write changes, and it doesn't poll LabArchives for new content. The import is a one-way snapshot, and you can re-run it whenever you have a fresher ZIP.

Several wizard-step screenshots are pending capture. Steps 2 (Upload) and 5 (Fetch images) require an actual notebook ZIP and a signed-in LabArchives session, neither of which the wiki-capture fixture seeds. Steps 3 (Preview notebook), 4 (Map projects), and the bulk-sort scree...
Step 1, only the Offline Notebook ZIP path is live right now.
The import wizard's Choose format step, with three cards: LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP selected in blue, and two grayed-out Coming soon cards for the PDF and Chrome print paths.

entries.title: PI (Principal Investigator)
headings: The six phases of the PI feature
bodySnippets: A PI is a per-user role: an account flagged with account_type === 'lab_head' that gains a small superset of affordances over a regular member. PI is not a separate user pool, it is a property on a normal account. One or more lab members fill this role (typical labs have a sing...

The role is a single flag on a user's metadata: , with values (the default) or . A lab folder can have one or more accounts flagged as PI; co-PI labs simply set the flag on every PI. A PI is otherwise a normal account: they have their own projects, tasks, and notes. The role d...

Picking a PI is a deliberate decision the lab makes. Usually the principal investigator who actually runs the lab fills the role, but it can be anyone: a senior postdoc, a lab manager, a designated person who triages purchases. The role does not assume any particular real-worl...
The PI role shipped in six tracked phases. Each one added a capability:

On the user picker, the PI shows up with a small badge and is pinned to the top of the list, regardless of which account is set as the "main user." This makes it easy to find the PI account on a shared lab laptop without scrolling.
The badge is also visible on comment rows wherever the PI posted: a small chip next to their name so members can tell when feedback is coming from the PI versus from another member.
Set one account as the PI when:
Set no PI (every account stays ) when:
Account type + comment attribution. Introduced , the picker badge, and the "posted by PI" styling on comment rows.
Comment threading + @-mentions. Built out the comment surface across tasks, notes, and purchases, with one-level threading and the denormalized mentions index. See Comments .
Soft-write actions. Purchase approval, task assignment, and flag-for-review affordances. See Soft-write actions .
Cross-lab metrics. Member workload, recent activity, burn rate; the dashboard data feeds the Lab Overview widget canvas.
Edit-session unlock + audit log. A password-gated 5-minute session per "Request Edit" affordance, with every soft-write writing to . See Edit session and password and Audit log .
User archiving. Remove a former member from active views while preserving their data. See User archiving .
Your lab has more than two or three people and you want a single person triaging purchases instead of everyone approving their own.
You need a forensic trail of who approved what (the audit log is gated on the PI role).
You want a customizable Lab Overview dashboard instead of every member working from the same Home page.
The lab is a single person, or two people who trust each other to manage their own purchases.
You do not need the audit log or the approval gate.
Edit session and password covers the Phase 5 unlock pattern.
Soft-write actions walks the approval queue, task assignment, and flag-for-review.
Audit log covers and how to read it.
The Lab Overview dashboard surface is gated on the PI role.

A PI has implicit read access to every record in the lab, regardless of . The permission system still respects the explicit sharing arrays for writes, but for reads, the PI sees everything. This is what makes the Lab Overview dashboards possible (the lab-wide member workload w...
The PI is pinned to the top of the user picker and carries a small role badge.
The user-picker showing two accounts, the PI pinned at the top with a small badge next to their avatar.

entries.title: Audit log
headings: How to read it (PiActions popup, tab 3)
bodySnippets: Every PI soft-write writes a row to _pi_audit.json. The log is the forensic trail of the lab: who approved which purchase, who flagged which note, who archived which user. The data is on disk in plain JSON, and the PiActions popup gives you a sortable view on top of it. Use it...
Every soft-write affordance appends a row. The captured fields per row are:

The lab-level audit log is at at the root of the lab folder, alongside the shared funding accounts and lab metadata. Per-user audit entries also append to when the action targets a record owned by that user. The two views serve different purposes:
Both files are append-only on the write side. The reader is free to sort and filter the rows however the UI needs.
Open the PI Actions popup from the Lab Overview (its tile, or via the Tools launcher) and switch to the third tab, Audit log . The tab renders the lab-level file as a sortable table:
The audit log is the place to go when:
actor : the PI username who performed the action.
action : a string identifier like , , , , , , , , , , .
target : the affected record id (purchase id, task id, note id, announcement id, or username).
field_path : a dotted path into the record showing which field changed, when the action mutates a field (e.g. , , ).
old_value : the value before the change. Null for create-type actions (post announcement).
new_value : the value after the change. Null for delete-type actions.
timestamp : ISO-8601 UTC timestamp.
Lab-level : every soft-write across the whole lab, read by the PiActions popup's Audit log tab.
Per-user : every soft-write touching this user's records, visible on their own profile surface so members can audit what was done to their work.
Default sort : timestamp descending (most recent first).
Filter chips : by action category (approvals, flags, announcements, user-mgmt), by actor, and by date range.
Click a row to expand the field path and the old / new value inline. For longer values (announcement bodies, for example) the expansion shows a diff-style view.
A purchase shows up as approved and the member needs to confirm who signed off and when (audit by target id).
An announcement was edited or deleted and you want to see the original (audit row carries the old body).
A member was archived months ago and you want to know which PI archived them and why (paired with the announcement or comment they left at the time).
The lab is preparing for a compliance review and needs a flat export of who approved which lab purchases over a date range (filter chips plus the JSON file underneath).

Like every other ResearchOS file, the audit log sits in plaintext on disk. Anyone with read access to the lab folder can read it directly. The point of the log is accountability across PI sessions, not protection against an adversary on the file system. See Security for the da...

The writer never modifies an existing row, only appends. So a PI cannot quietly "edit" an old audit entry to hide what they did. If a row looks wrong, the right move is to write a new action that corrects it (re-approve a wrongly-declined purchase, for example) so both rows ar...
The Audit log tab. Each row is one soft-write, with the field path that changed and the before / after values.
The Audit log tab of the PI Actions popup, showing a chronological list of soft-write rows with actor, action, target, field path, and old/new values.

entries.title: Edit session and password
headings: Other soft-write affordances that use the same session
bodySnippets: A PI's soft-write actions (approve a purchase, post an announcement, archive a user) are gated by a short edit session. You unlock the session once with the PI password, get a 5-minute window during which the affordances become active, and the session auto-expires after. This ...

Soft-write actions are different from a regular edit. A purchase approval, an announcement, or a user archive is a lab-wide signal that other people will act on (the member sees their purchase approved and orders it, the whole lab reads the announcement and plans around it). A...

The model is the same as elevating to admin on a normal OS: the password proves intent, not identity. Anyone with the file system already has access to your data folder (see Security ); the gate is about catching a misclick, not about defending against an adversary.

The PI password is set the first time you click any soft-write affordance on a fresh PI account. The dialog flips to a "set a new password" mode that asks you to type and confirm. After the first unlock, the dialog reverts to a single password field for ongoing sessions.

The PI password is stored separately from the per-user account password (the one you use to sign in to ResearchOS, documented on Creating a user ). Using two distinct passwords keeps the gates orthogonal: someone leaning over your shoulder while you sign in does not also get t...
Any locked soft-write affordance shows a Request Edit button (or, on inline affordances, the action itself prompts on click). The unlock flow is:

After 5 minutes of inactivity (no soft-write actions) the session auto-locks. Any open dialog stays mounted but the action buttons re-disable, so an in-progress edit is not destroyed mid-keystroke, you just have to unlock again to commit. Each soft-write also extends the timer...

You can also lock the session manually from the header (a small lock icon next to the timer). Useful when you are stepping away from a shared lab laptop and want to make sure the next person to sit down does not have your edit window open.
The same 5-minute session covers every PI soft-write:

Every one of these writes a row to . The audit happens whether or not you unlocked the session by manual prompt or by an earlier action within the 5-minute window, so the trail is complete. See Audit log .
Click Request Edit .
Type the PI password in the dialog. On success, the dialog closes and a 5-minute countdown starts.
The affordances unlock for the duration of the session. Approve a purchase, post an announcement, archive a member, all without re-prompting.
The session timer is visible in the Lab Overview header so you always know how long you have left.
Purchase approval / decline. See Purchases .
Task assignment. Assigning a member to a task you are looking at.
Flag for review. Across tasks, notes, and purchases.
Announcements. See Announcements .
User archive / unarchive. See User archiving .

The PI password sidecar lives at the lab folder root. Delete it from Finder or Explorer and the next soft-write affordance will prompt for a fresh password instead. Your data is untouched. As with the account password, this is a deterrent on a shared machine, not encryption.
The Request Edit dialog. Type the PI password, get a 5-minute session, the soft-write affordances unlock.
The Request Edit password dialog with a single password input, an Unlock button, and a remaining-time hint at the bottom.

entries.title: Soft-write actions
bodySnippets: A soft-write is a PI action that touches a record owned by someone else: approving a member's purchase, assigning a member to a task, flagging a note for review, posting an announcement. Every soft-write goes through the edit-session unlock and writes to the audit log. The pat...

Pending purchases show up in the Pending approvals tab of the PI Actions popup, on the Pending tile in the Lab Overview sidebar, and inline on the Purchases page as a yellow stripe. Each row has two inline buttons:

A declined purchase can be re-approved later from the same Recently declined section (a small Re-approve link on the row). This is useful when the original decline reason resolves (the member finds funding for the line item, the vendor sorts out a stock issue) without forcing ...

On any task popup the PI sees an Assign to member control above the description. Pick a member from the dropdown and the task's field updates. The audit row captures the actor (PI), the target (task id), and the old / new assignee.

Assigning a task does not move ownership. The original creator still owns the file on disk; the assignment is just a soft signal that surfaces on the assignee's home page and in the member workload widget on the Lab Overview.
A PI can flag any task, note, or purchase as needing review. The affordance is a small Flag button on the host record popup. Flagged items show up:

Flags are intentionally lightweight: there is no required comment or category, just a single bit that says "this needs another look." The member resolves the flag by clicking Resolve on their copy, which writes a resolve event to the audit trail.
Announcements are a lab-wide soft-write covered in its own page. Same edit-session unlock, same audit row. See Announcements .

Every soft-write goes through the same 5-minute edit-session unlock. The first soft-write in a cold session prompts for the PI password, the next four-and-a-half minutes worth of actions run without re-prompting. See Edit session and password for the unlock flow and security r...
Approve. Flips the purchase's status to approved and writes an audit row. The member sees the approval on their own Purchases page on the next refresh.
Decline. Flips the status to declined, sets a timestamp, and the purchase moves into the Recently declined section of the Pending tab. Declined purchases render with a red wherever they appear.
In the Flagged tab of the PI Actions popup.
On the member's own copy of the record, with a red stripe and a hint.
In the Lab Activity stream as a flag-event row.

Soft-writes never overwrite a member's own work without their knowledge. The member always sees the result (the approved purchase, the assigned task, the flag) on their own page. The PI can signal, not silently take over. If a member needs a record taken away from them entirel...
PI Actions, Pending tab. Inline Approve / Decline per row, plus a Recently declined section with a Re-approve affordance.
The PI Actions popup showing the Pending Approvals tab with a list of waiting purchases and a Recently declined section at the bottom.

entries.title: Lab Inbox
headings: How it relates to the bell
bodySnippets: The Lab Inbox is the lab-wide activity surface for everything anyone said to anyone else. Comments on tasks, notes, and purchases all land here, as do @-mentions and lab-wide announcements. It is the asynchronous chat layer of ResearchOS: the place you check first thing in the...

Lab Inbox is universal. Every lab member (and every PI) sees the same inbox surface, scoped by what they have read access to. Comments on a private task only show up for the people who can see that task; announcements posted to the whole lab show up for everyone. The permissio...

The Lab Inbox and the bell-icon notification dropdown are siblings with different scopes. The bell is your personal queue: shared-with-me, reminders, shift alerts, things you specifically need to acknowledge. The Lab Inbox is the lab's ambient chatter: a place to drop a commen...
See Notifications and Inbox for the bell, the Telegram image inbox, and the calendar reminders that live in the personal queue.
Comments. Threaded replies on tasks, notes, and purchases. One-level reply nesting (a comment plus its direct replies). See Comments for the data shape and @-mention behavior.

Announcements. Lab-wide posts from a PI. They sit in chronological order by default. A PI can pin one to keep it at the top of the feed, and the pin persists until they explicitly unpin it. See Announcements .

@-mentions. A filtered view of comments where you were called out by name. The mention array is denormalized (see the Comments page for the why), so the filter is a fast index lookup, not a body regex.
Comments covers threading, mentions, and the in-place source-record popup.
Announcements covers posting, pinning, edit-session unlock, and the audit trail.
For the Lab Overview surface that hosts the Lab Inbox Tool, see Lab Overview .

Comments are visible to everyone with read access to the host record, not just the record's owner. So if a PI reads a member's task and leaves a comment, the next time another member with read access (via sharing) opens that task, they see the comment in context. The inbox is ...
The Lab Inbox in one stream: comments, @-mentions, and announcements all flow through here.
The Lab Inbox popup showing a mix of comments, @-mentions, and announcement rows in a single chronological stream.

entries.title: Announcements
headings: When to post an announcement vs. a comment
Audit lives at the lab root
bodySnippets: Announcements are the PI's broadcast channel: short posts visible to every member, pinned to the top of the lab activity surface until they age out. Members read; PIs write. Writing is gated by an edit-session unlock so an accidental keystroke does not push something out to th...

Posting is PI only. The compose form does not render for member accounts at all, and the underlying API rejects writes from any account without . Reading is universal: every member sees the same announcement stream.
A PI cannot post an announcement directly from a cold session. The flow requires a short edit-session unlock first:
The 5-minute window matches the rest of the PI soft-write pattern. Cross-link to Edit session and password for the full unlock walkthrough and the security reasoning.
Each announcement carries three actions the author can take after publishing:
Only the author can edit or delete an announcement. A second PI cannot edit another PI's post (this would muddy the audit trail), only post their own follow-up.

The compose form persists in-progress drafts to as you type, so an accidental tab close or refresh does not lose your unposted announcement. The draft restores automatically when you next mount the compose form. It is per-Lab-Head and per-tab; it does not survive into a fresh ...

Every announcement write (post, edit, delete, pin, unpin) appends a row to at the lab folder root. The row records the actor (which PI), the action, the affected announcement id, the old and new values for edits, and the timestamp. See Audit log for the full schema and the PiA...
Announcements and comments are both async, but they answer different questions:
Click Request Edit . A small password dialog opens.
Type the PI password (the one you set in Settings, separate from the per-user account password).

On success, an edit session opens for 5 minutes. The compose form becomes active, and any other soft-write affordances (approve a purchase, post an announcement, archive a user) work without re-prompting for the duration.
Pin / Unpin. A pinned announcement stays at the top of the stream regardless of post date. Unpin to let it slide back into chronological order.
Edit. Open the original compose form pre-filled with the title and body, make changes, save. The edit is logged to the audit trail (see below).
Delete. Removes the announcement after a confirmation. The deletion is logged with the original body so you can recover an accidentally-deleted post from the audit trail.

Post an announcement when the message is for the whole lab, persists for at least a few days, and benefits from being on the lab's shared front page. Examples: "Freezer is down, do not store anything in -80B," "Lab meeting moved to Thursday," "Equipment ordering deadline is Fr...

Post a comment when the message is about a specific record (a task, a note, a purchase). The comment stays tied to its host, surfaces in the Lab Inbox stream alongside the record context, and does not need to clutter the front page. See Comments .

Unlike per-user soft writes (purchase approvals, task assignments) that have both a lab-level and a per-user audit entry, announcements only have a lab-level entry because they are inherently lab-scoped. There is no per-user view of announcement edits.
The compose form. Title plus body. Pin keeps the announcement at the top of the stream until you unpin it.
The Announcements composer with a title, body, and a Pin checkbox, visible after the PI unlocked an edit session.

entries.title: Comments
bodySnippets: Comments are short asynchronous messages attached to a record (a task, a note, or a purchase). They keep a conversation tied to the thing it is about: a question about a PCR recipe lives on that PCR task, not in a separate chat channel. One level of reply nesting keeps threads...
Three record types accept comments today:

Open any of those records and the comments panel sits at the bottom of the popup. The Lab Inbox is the aggregator: the same comments appear in the inbox stream so you can scan everything written today without opening each record one at a time.

Comments support a single level of reply nesting. A top-level comment can have replies; a reply cannot have its own reply. This keeps a thread legible at a glance and prevents the rabbit-hole shape that threaded chat platforms drift into. If a reply itself needs a deeper conve...
Type inside a comment to summon the mention picker. Pick a lab member from the dropdown and the editor inserts a mention chip. The mention does two things:
Members see their @-mentions both in the bell (the personal queue) and in the @-mentions filter of the Lab Inbox.

Clicking a comment row in the Lab Inbox opens the host record in a popup on top of the inbox. The inbox itself stays mounted underneath, so closing the host popup drops you straight back into the inbox at the same scroll position. This is the Lab Inbox R1 affordance: you can t...
The same affordance works the other way around. Open a task from any other surface (Gantt, Workbench, Home), scroll to the comments panel, and the same comment object is there with the same thread.

Comments inherit the read permission of their host record. If you have read access to a task that a labmate owns, you also see (and can post) comments on it. PIs have implicit read access to everything, so they see every comment in the lab. Members only see comments on the rec...
