Tasks (experiments, lists, purchase orders).
Notes (free-form lab notes outside an experiment).
Purchases (individual line items inside a purchase order).
Renders in the comment body as a styled chip (the member's name, in the member's color).
Pushes the member's id onto the comment's array, a denormalized field that the inbox uses to filter for "comments I was mentioned in."

Earlier prototypes filtered mentions by regex-matching the comment body. That fell apart with edits, mention chip styling, and special characters. The denormalized array is now the only source of truth for "was X mentioned." The body rendering is independent: it can change lay...

Comments are the asynchronous-chat layer of ResearchOS: low-friction, in-context, no hard notification unless you @-mention someone. If you need someone to see something right now, mention them and the bell pings. Otherwise, drop the comment and trust that the recipient will f...
A thread on a task: top-level comments with one level of reply indent. @-mentions render as inline chips.
A comments thread inside a task popup showing two top-level comments, one with an indented reply, and an @-mention chip inside one of the bodies.

entries.title: Lab Overview
bodySnippets: The Lab Overview at /lab-overview is the PI's customizable dashboard for the whole lab. It is not a separate view of the same app; it is a canvas of small, draggable summary tiles that each click out into a full popup. Members do not see this surface at all (their starting poi...

Lab Overview is gated on . Lab members never see it. After the Home canvas migration, members land on for their own work, while PIs get this dedicated cross-lab surface at . The two surfaces share the widget vocabulary but answer different questions: Home asks "what is on my p...

PIs also get a Home canvas of their own (with its own default layout under ). Lab Overview is not their only widget surface; it is the cross-lab dashboard that sits next to a personal Home, and the two layouts persist separately.
The page has three regions you interact with:
Once these three primitives click, the rest of the page is just composition.

The snapshot canvas is the main grid in the center. Each cell is a widget tile that pulls live data from the lab and renders a compact summary. Tiles are draggable when edit mode is on, toggled via the Edit layout text button in the canvas toolbar (right side of the same row a...

The customizable sidebar runs down the right edge as a vertical rail of slim tiles. Pin a widget to the sidebar when you want it always-visible without consuming canvas space, the way you would put a battery indicator in a phone status bar.

The Tools launcher is a header button that opens the same widget popups directly, without pinning anything. Useful when you want a one-shot look at audit logs or pending purchases but do not want a permanent tile for it.

Tools. The canonical full-screen popup for each subject (announcements, comments, purchases, member workload, and so on). Each Tool is the same popup regardless of where you opened it from: a tile click, the Tools launcher, or a sidebar entry all route to the same surface. See...

Widget variants. A single Tool can have multiple tile shapes. , for example, ships three variants (funding-bars, burn-rate, pending-count); each one summarizes the same underlying data in a different visual register. Think of it as the iPhone-widgets model: same app, different...

Customizable layout. Edit mode (toggle from the gear icon) flips every tile into drag mode. Snapshots reorder freely, the sidebar rail accepts drops from the canvas and vice versa, and a + Add widget palette mounts a panel of every available widget. Reset to default restores t...
Widgets and Tools covers the 12 Tools you can pin and the variant system.
Customizable sidebar walks the right-edge rail (PIs only).
Snapshot tiles and expanded views explains the tile-to-popup model, drag-to-reorder, and edit mode.
For the PI role itself (the account type that gates this whole page), see PI .

Every widget conforms to the same contract on the data side and renders both a small snapshot body and a full expanded popup. That is what makes the variants pluggable: pinning a widget to the sidebar or dragging it onto the canvas is the same underlying object, just rendered ...
The Lab Overview. Snapshot tiles in the main grid, a customizable sidebar rail on the right, and a Tools header button up top.
The Lab Overview at /lab-overview, showing a grid of widget tiles (announcements, comments, lab activity) with a vertical sidebar rail on the right pinning a few extra tiles.

entries.title: Customizable sidebar
headings: Pinning a widget to the rail
Widgets that ship with a sidebar variant
Click a sidebar tile to open the full Tool
bodySnippets: The Lab Overview has a second canvas: a vertical rail down the right edge that holds slim, always-visible tiles. It is the place to put things you want a constant ambient read on (today's tasks, today's announcements, pending approvals) without surrendering canvas real estate....

The snapshot canvas in the middle of the Lab Overview is great for widgets you want at a comfortable reading size, but it has a finite height before you start scrolling. The sidebar rail solves the scroll problem for the small handful of widgets you want in your peripheral vis...

The Lab Overview's customizable rail has its own edit toggle that is separate from the snapshot canvas's Edit layout button. With the rail's edit mode on, drag a tile out of the snapshot canvas and drop it on the sidebar rail. The rail accepts the drop and the tile re-renders ...
You can also pin straight from the palette. Open + Add widget , find the Tool you want, and drop the sidebar variant directly on the rail without first staging it on the canvas.
Inside the rail, drag any pinned tile up or down to change the stacking order. The order persists per PI (it lives in your settings sidecar), so each PI's rail is their own.
Not every Tool has a slim variant. The ones that do are the ones a PI usually wants at a glance:

Every widget that supports the rail conforms to the contract, which is the slim-rendering side of the larger widget contract. A widget without a SidebarTile implementation never offers a sidebar variant in the palette, so the rail only ever shows tiles that are designed to fit...

Daily tasks. Today's tasks across the lab, ranked. The sidebar variant is the same data as the canvas version with the per-row chrome trimmed. Pinning daily tasks here frees the canvas for week-long widgets.
Today's announcements. A compressed read of the Announcements Tool that only surfaces pinned and same-day announcements. Cross-link to Announcements for how those get written.
PI actions. A small queue of pending approvals and flagged items, with the tile body acting as a quick-action button. See Soft-write actions .
Member workload. A condensed list of members with their active and overdue counts.

Recent activity (sidebar). A condensed slice of the rolling lab activity feed, rendered as one-liners. (Not the same as the full Lab activity Tool; that one is canvas / home only and does not pin to the rail.)
Daily tasks variants. Daily tasks ships , , and as three time-window-specific slim rail tiles, plus a full-stack variant that combines all three. Pin whichever window you actually want to glance at.

The Lab Overview surface itself is gated on . Members never have a page, so the sidebar rail never appears for them either. The closest analog on the member side is the home canvas at , which has its own widget layout but no permanent right-edge rail. (The page uses the existi...
Sidebar tiles are still tiles. Clicking one opens the same Tool popup you would get from the canvas, so the rail is also your one-click path to the full surface for the things you watch most often.
The right-edge rail. Each tile is a slim sidebar variant of a Tool, always visible while you work on the canvas.
The right-edge sidebar rail on the Lab Overview, showing three slim widget tiles pinned vertically.

entries.title: Snapshot tiles and expanded views
headings: Sidebar tiles and canvas tiles are the same widget
bodySnippets: Every widget on the Lab Overview is two things at once: a small snapshot tile that lives on the canvas, and a richer expanded view that opens as a popup when you click the tile. The split is what lets the page stay dense without losing the full story behind any one number.
Each widget renders at two fidelities:
Clicking a tile is the only way to open its expanded view. The Tools launcher in the page header opens the same popups directly, without needing a tile.

With edit mode on (toggled via the Edit layout text button in the canvas toolbar at the top-right of the canvas itself, NOT a gear icon), tiles sprout drag handles and the canvas turns into a grid you can rearrange. Drag a tile to a new cell, drop it, and the layout persists i...

Edit mode is sticky until you toggle it off (the button switches to Done while active), so you can stage a whole new layout in one pass. The page is interactive the rest of the time; tile clicks still open the expanded view while edit mode is off. One detail worth knowing: cli...

The canvas renders tiles in a fixed responsive grid (one column on narrow viewports, two columns on desktop). Tiles are uniform in their grid cell; there is no per-tile size selector or resize handle. The layout shape stored in your settings sidecar is an ordered list of widge...

The Reset button in the canvas toolbar (sibling to Edit layout and +Add widget, NOT a menu item) restores the shipping layout: the default widget set, in the default positions, with the default variants. Your customizations wipe in one click. Useful when you have experimented ...
Every Tool popup follows the same shape:
The popup is where every click-out from the canvas lands, so it is worth getting comfortable with the shared shape. The widget tiles are the entry points; the popup is where the work happens.

The snapshot tile on the canvas (or in the sidebar rail) shows the single most important fact: a count, a tiny chart, a single bar. Tiles are designed to be readable without interaction, so a glance at the page tells you the state of the lab.
The expanded view is the full Tool popup. It opens centered over the page and contains every tab, control, filter, and action the Tool supports. Same data, full surface.
A header bar with the Tool name, the close button, and any global controls (time range pickers, search boxes).
One or more tabs when the Tool has distinct sub-views (PI actions has Pending / Flagged / Audit log, for example).
A body with the rich data: tables, charts, lists, inline edit affordances.
Per-row actions where the Tool supports them. Approve a purchase from the Pending tab, mark a flagged note resolved, etc.

Drag a tile from the canvas onto the right-edge sidebar rail and it re-renders in its slim sidebar variant. Drag it back and it pops back into its canvas variant. The Tool behind the tile does not change, only the rendering. See Customizable sidebar for the rail-side details.
The tile shows the headline. Click it and the same data opens in the full Tool popup with every tab and control available.
A small burn-rate widget tile on the canvas with the full LabPurchases popup mounted on top, showing the same data at two different fidelities.

entries.title: Widgets and Tools
headings: When the popup title follows the tile
bodySnippets: A Tool is the full popup. A Widget is the tile that opens it. The Lab Overview ships 13 Tools, each tile has one or more visual variants, and the Tools launcher in the header opens any Tool without you needing a pinned tile for it. This page walks the full catalog and the vari...

Each Tool is the canonical popup for one subject. Clicking any of its tiles, or picking it from the Tools launcher in the header, opens the same surface. They are the heart of the Lab Overview, the rest of the page is just how you reach them.
Some Tools come in more than one tile shape because the same data deserves different visual treatments depending on what you are glancing for. is the clearest example:

You pick a variant from the palette when you drag a widget onto the canvas. Multiple variants of the same Tool can co-exist on the page, each showing the same underlying data through a different lens. Click any of them and the expanded popup opens; some variants label their he...

Most Tools host more than one sibling widget. The Daily Tasks Tool, for example, has four tiles that share the same body: an Overdue slice, a Today slice, an Upcoming slice, and a full-stack version that bundles all three. The popup body is identical no matter which tile you c...

Today four variants carry a per-tile header label: shows "Today's events", shows "Overdue tasks", shows "Upcoming tasks", and shows "Daily tasks". The override is set per variant, so a Tool whose sibling tiles don't need their own header fall back to the Tool's umbrella title ...
A handful of other Tools also ship in multiple variants beyond the canonical tile:

The Tools button in the page header opens a flat menu of every Tool. Pick one and the canonical popup opens. The Tools launcher is the right path when you want a one-shot look at something you do not want pinned to the canvas. Audit log is a common example: most days you do no...

The + Add widget button lives in the canvas toolbar at the TOP of the canvas (NOT a gear icon, NOT at the bottom). Clicking it slides in a panel showing every available widget, grouped by Tool, with the tile variants listed beside each one. Drag a tile from the palette onto th...

Already-pinned widgets are marked in the palette so you can still pin a second copy of the same variant if you want (a vendor breakdown by burn-rate at one size and the same widget at another, for example).

With edit mode on, hover a tile and a remove handle appears in the corner. Click it to unpin the widget. The Tool itself stays available from the Tools launcher and the palette; only this specific tile is gone. Reset to default restores the shipping layout in one click.
Announcements. Pinned and recent announcements for the whole lab. Read for everyone, write for PIs (with an edit-session unlock). See Announcements .
Comments. The lab-wide comment stream across tasks, notes, and purchases, with @-mention filtering and threaded replies. See Comments .
Notes. Recently updated lab notes you have read access to. Click a row to open the note popup.
Experiments. The lab-wide experiment list grouped by member, with each row clicking out to the experiment popup.
Purchases. The pending-approval queue, funding-bar summaries, and the burn-rate trend. Has three tile variants (described below).
Metrics. Cross-lab counts: total active tasks, purchases pending, recent shared notes, and so on.
Daily tasks. Today's tasks across every member. Pinnable to the sidebar as a slim, always-visible variant.
Lab activity. The rolling activity feed: who saved what, who marked what complete, who shared what.
Recent activity. A focused, smaller-window view of lab activity for the snapshot canvas.
PI actions. The PI's soft-write console: pending approvals, flagged items, and the audit log. See Soft-write actions .
Member workload. Per-member counts of active tasks, overdue items, and flagged work, so you can see who is overloaded at a glance.
Today's announcements. A leaner variant of Announcements that only surfaces what is pinned or posted today, good as a sidebar tile.

Calendar. A popup view of today's and upcoming lab calendar events. Backs the tile variant that shows the next chunk of events in a compact list. Pinning that variant is how the Home canvas' default "Today's events" tile gets its data.
Funding bars. A horizontal bar per funding account showing spent vs. budget. Good for "how much grant is left."
Burn rate. A small trend chart of weekly spend with a range selector (4w / 8w / 12w / 6mo). Good for "are we accelerating."
Pending count. A single big number plus the top few waiting items. Good for "do I need to approve something right now."
Comments → comment-mentions. The same Comments stream, filtered to just @-mentions of you. Good for "what requires my response."

Experiments → experiments-ready-writeup. The Experiments list filtered to entries that are ready for a write-up (data captured, results section empty). Good for a PI's "what needs my eye for publication."
Lab activity → lab-activity-by-type. Splits the activity feed into today's tasks, notes, and purchases as three side-by-side columns instead of one rolling stream.
Calendar → calendar-events-today. A compact today-only events list, the default Home widget variant.
Daily tasks → sidebar variants. Daily tasks ships , , , and a full-stack variant for the customizable sidebar rail (PIs only). All four are visually slim versions of the canvas tile.

Think of variants as views, not separate widgets. If you change the time range on the burn-rate variant, that range applies to that tile only. The pending-count variant ignores it because pending-count does not have a time dimension.
The + Add widget palette. Each card shows one Tool plus the available tile variants (small, medium, large) you can pin to the canvas.
The Lab Overview widget palette open over the canvas, listing every Tool with their tile variants.

entries.title: Links / Lab Links
headings: Q7 opt-in: Links is only visible if you enabled it
bodySnippets: Your own bookmark wall: link cards with preview images, grouped into categories, stored under your user.

Each saved link is a card with a preview image (or a solid colored block if no image), the link title, a short description, and the hostname. Cards are grouped into category sections. The section header is the category name, and links without a category land under Other . Clic...

Hover a card. Two small icons appear in the top-right corner of the preview image: a pencil to edit (reopens the same form pre-filled), and a trash can to delete (prompts for confirmation). The hostname line at the bottom of each card is auto-derived from the URL. You only eve...

Per the on-disk JSON: a title, a URL, an optional description, an optional category, a color, and an optional preview image URL. That's it. There is no per-link sharing toggle, no cross-user visibility, no labmate ordering. If a labmate needs the same set of URLs, they add the...
Click Add Link in the top right of the page. A form expands inline below the button.

Paste a URL into the URL field. Click the small image-icon button next to it to fetch a preview. That fills in the title, description, and preview image automatically (the fields are still editable afterward).

Give the link a Title (required), pick or type a Category (the dropdown suggests Protocol, Database, Tool, Reference, Supplier, Publication, Software, Other, but any text works), and pick one of the eight color swatches.
Optionally add a Description and override the Preview Image URL with a custom image.
Click Create Link . The new card appears in its category.
Solo accounts see this surface labeled "Links" in the nav and page header. Lab accounts see "Lab Links". The feature is identical either way.
The Links tab only appears if you answered "Yes" to the setup wizard question about storing bookmarks (Q7). If you don't see the tab, open Settings and re-run the setup wizard to enable it.
The color swatch only appears when no preview image is set. If you supply both a color and a preview image URL, the image takes precedence and the color is not shown.
Links sit in your own folder under . Each labmate keeps their own wall. Adding a link doesn't share it with anyone else. There is no per-link public or shared toggle in the data model.
Link cards grouped by category. Each card opens its URL in a new tab when clicked.
The Links page showing link cards grouped into category sections, each card with a preview image and a title underneath.

entries.title: The Markdown Editor
headings: What a real ResearchOS note looks like
No metadata popup on file tiles
Paste tables from Excel or Sheets
bodySnippets: Wherever you write prose in ResearchOS (experiment notes, task descriptions, results write-ups, methods bodies, free-form notes), you're using the same markdown editor. Learning its two modes and shortcut set pays off fast.
The same editor opens in every place you write more than a sentence in ResearchOS:

Everything you type is plain markdown. The toolbar buttons just insert the same characters you'd type by hand (e.g., , ), so opening any of these files in a normal text editor outside ResearchOS gets you the same content.
The toolbar in the top-right of every editor has a two-way mode toggle. The choice of mode is purely a viewing preference, it doesn't change what gets saved.
Hybrid is the default and where most editing happens. The editor parses your markdown into logical blocks (paragraphs, headings, code blocks, lists, tables) and each block is a click target:
Other Hybrid-mode shortcuts on a selected (but not yet editing) block:
Use Cmd on macOS and Ctrl on Windows and Linux anywhere this table says Cmd .

Images are first-class. Every editor has an attachments area along the bottom (toggleable from the toolbar) with two tabs, Images and Files . The Images tab shows thumbnails for every image in the document's folder, whether or not the body references the image yet. There are f...

Every image lands in an folder adjacent to the document on disk (e.g., ), and the markdown body references it with . Filenames with spaces render inline just fine. The reference looks like and the editor resolves it to the right file without any extra escaping.

The Images tab at the bottom of the editor lists every image in the document's folder as a row of thumbnails. Images already referenced in the body look normal. Images that exist on disk but aren't referenced yet (e.g., a fresh arrival from Telegram) show a small blue dot in t...

Click any rendered image in Hybrid or Preview mode and a size popover appears inline with 25% , 50% , 75% , and 100% options. The choice writes a width attribute into the markdown so the size persists.

If an image reference points at a file that no longer exists (e.g., you renamed the file outside the app, or the relink hasn't synced from a teammate yet), a small red "Image Not Found" popup appears in the bottom-right corner of the editor. It lists similar-named files in the...

Anything that isn't an image (PDFs, CSVs, sequence files, protocols, archives) drops into a sibling folder and shows up as a clickable hyperlink in the prose, not as an inline preview. The flow mirrors the image flow: drag from Finder, paste, or pick from the toolbar.
Click any link in Hybrid or Preview mode and a small View / Download popup appears centered on screen.

When a link points at a file that isn't in the document's folder, the same red corner popup that handles broken images opens with a File Not Found heading. There's no similar-name search for files (the recovery is usually to remove the dead link), so the popup goes straight to...
A typical note in a working lab is mostly tables, measurements, and small annotations. The four examples below are the shape to aim for.
Standard GitHub-flavored markdown all works:

The Hybrid editor includes a collapsible helper panel on the left side of the editor with two tabs at the top, Shortcuts and Style Guide . Click the arrow in the panel header to collapse or expand it.
The editor doesn't autosave on every keystroke. The parent surface (the experiment popup, the task popup, the results page) decides when to flush to disk. As a rule of thumb:

On save, the editor also runs a quick cleanup pass over the document's and folders: anything sitting on disk that nothing in the markdown points at gets deleted, so deleted snippets don't leave dangling files behind. The sweep matches links the way the body writes them, so URL...
The Lab Notes tab of any experiment popup (see Experiments & Notes ).
The Description field on the task detail popup, which you can open from the Gantt , the left sidebar, search results, or the calendar.
The Results tab inside the Workbench project view (the old standalone Results page was retired; now redirects to Workbench ).
The body of every method in the Methods library .
Free-form notes (running logs and standalone notes).

Hybrid (the default): the rendered view, but every block is click-to-edit. Single-click a paragraph or heading to select it, double-click (or Enter ) to edit just that block, and Esc to commit and deselect. This is the right mode for most prose work.
Preview : read-only rendered output. Click any image in this mode to bring up the resize picker. Use it when you're sharing the screen or reviewing.
Delete or Backspace removes the block.
Esc deselects.
Click the toolbar's image button to pick one or more files from disk.

Drag image files into the editor body . While you're dragging from Finder, a blue ring lights up around the popup or editor card so you know the drop will be caught. Release inside text and the image inserts at that position. Release outside text and it appends to the bottom o...
Paste from the clipboard . Copy a screenshot (e.g., macOS Cmd + Shift + 4 , then Cmd + V in the editor) and it lands inline.
Click Browse to pick from images already attached to this experiment (the gallery picker). Useful when the image is already on disk and you just want to insert a reference to it without re-uploading.

Click a thumbnail to open the image metadata popup. The popup shows a larger preview and lets you edit the caption, rename the file, delete it from disk, or jump to where it's used in the body. If the image isn't referenced anywhere yet, the jump button is disabled.
Drag a thumbnail into the editor to insert that image at the cursor position. The thumbnail stays in the tab so the same image can appear twice in the document.

Drag a thumbnail to the trash zone at the bottom of the editor. ResearchOS deletes the file from disk and removes every reference in the body. The trash zone only appears while you are actively dragging a thumbnail.

Click the toolbar's Add File button to pick one or more non-image files from disk. The button appears in place of Add Image when the editor is configured to accept any file type (experiment Lab Notes, Results, and Notes). The chosen file copies into and a markdown link inserts...

Drag a file from Finder anywhere over the editor. The blue ring lights up while the file is hovering. On release the file copies into and a markdown link inserts at the cursor (or appends to the document if you dropped outside text).

The Files tab sits next to the Images tab at the bottom of the editor. Click the Files tab to swap the strip from image thumbnails to file tiles. Each tile shows an emoji for the file type and the filename. Files already linked in the body look normal. Files that exist on disk...

Drag a file tile to the red trash zone at the bottom-right of the editor. ResearchOS asks for confirmation, then deletes the file from disk and strips every link to it from the markdown body, including links that stored the filename URL-encoded (so gets cleaned up when the und...

Text-like files (markdown, txt, csv, json, code, sequence files like fasta and gbk) get a popup with Cancel , Download , and View buttons. Click View and the contents render in an inline monospace viewer with its own Download button up top. Click Download from either spot and ...
PDFs get the same popup. Click View and ResearchOS opens the PDF in a new browser tab so you can use the browser's built-in PDF viewer. Click Download and a copy lands on disk.
Everything else (zips, office docs, audio, video, binaries) downloads immediately without a popup. There's nothing meaningful to render inline.
Tables with the -and- syntax.
Bulleted ( ) and numbered ( ) lists.
Task lists with and . Boxes are clickable in Hybrid and Preview.
Blockquotes ( ).
Horizontal rules ( ).
Inline code with single backticks and code blocks with triple backticks.
Shortcuts lists every keyboard shortcut the editor responds to, with the key combo on the right. Read-only.

Style Guide shows example syntax for every markdown feature (headings, lists, tables, code blocks, callouts). Click any example to insert it at the cursor inside the active inline textarea. Handy when you've forgotten the table syntax or want to see what callout markdown looks...
Closing a popup (e.g., the task detail popup) saves first.
Switching to another task or experiment saves the current one.
Standalone editors (Results, Methods, Notes) save on a short debounce as you type. There's no "Save" button to hunt for.

Undo / redo across the whole document : the editor maintains its own undo stack (not the browser's textarea native undo). Cmd + Z steps back through edits, including block deletions and image drops. Cmd + Shift + Z (or Ctrl + Y on Windows) steps forward.
Promote/demote a heading in place with Cmd + Ctrl + + or Cmd + Ctrl + - . You don't need to retype the marks.

Split here : while editing a paragraph block inline, a Split here button appears at the cursor position. Clicking it is the same as pressing Shift + Enter : it inserts a hard paragraph break at that point, commits, and produces two separate blocks.
+ Add paragraph button at the bottom of the editor body. Click it to append a fresh empty paragraph block without scrolling or clicking in the body.
The Strip button in the toolbar toggles the attachments strip (Images and Files tabs) on and off. Click it again to bring the strip back. It hides both tabs at once.
Drag a thumbnail back into the editor to insert the same image a second time. The file isn't duplicated, only the reference is.

entries.title: Methods Library
headings: What you see on the page
Sharing a method with the lab
Attach a method to an experiment
Recording variations on a single experiment
Transient read access when a task is shared with you
bodySnippets: A method is a reusable protocol you write once and attach to any experiment as a tab. The library ships ten method types, from free-form Markdown to specialized structured editors for PCR, LC gradients, plate layouts, and more, so a method matches the shape of the work instead...

The Method Library page lists every protocol you and your lab have saved, grouped under category headings (e.g., "Molecular Biology", "Imaging"). Each method is a card with its name, a colored type pill (Markdown, PDF, PCR, LC Gradient, Plate Layout, and so on), and any tags y...
Two buttons sit in the top-right of the page: + New Category creates an empty category heading you can drag methods into later, and + New Method opens the create-method modal.
The create-method modal groups types into two sections. Each type gets its own viewer when the method is opened, its own icon on experiment tabs, and its own colored pill on the library card.
Standard methods are body-only (no structured fields beyond title and tags):
Structured methods swap the markdown body for a purpose-built editor:

Categories are flat (no sub-folders). To move a method into a different category, grab its card by the ⋮⋮ handle and drop it on another category heading. The drop target highlights blue while you hover. Drop a card on the "Drop here to move to Uncategorized" bar at the top to ...
Use + New Category to set up an empty bucket before you have anything to put in it. Empty categories persist in your browser until at least one method lives there or you remove it.

Every method is private to you by default. To make one visible to the rest of the lab, open the method and click the Private pill in the header. The share popup opens. In the User dropdown, select All Lab Users . A confirmation box appears below. Click Apply . The pill updates...
Lab-shared methods show a green Shared badge on their card and appear in every user's library.

Sharing is stored on the method's array. Choosing All Lab Users writes the value into the array, which the permission system expands to "every user in this lab folder, present and future." Read access is gated by the primitive: owner, anyone explicitly in , the sentinel, or a ...

Once a Markdown method is shared with the lab, the inline Edit button disappears for everyone, including the creator, and the body becomes read-only. To change a published Markdown protocol, flip it back to private via the share popup, edit, then republish.

Structured-method editors (PCR, LC Gradient, Plate Layout, and others) behave differently. The creator retains edit access even when the method is shared, because the editor is gated by (which passes for the original creator regardless of sharing status). Other lab members can...
All users can always attach a shared method to their own experiments and record per-run variations without touching the shared library copy.

There is one more way a method becomes readable: when a user shares a task with you and that task references a method, you get a transient read on the underlying protocol even if the method itself is not in your . The check lives in (in ) and the method owner sees a entry land...
Inside an experiment popup, methods appear as a row of browser-style tabs at the top of the Methods area. Click the + button on the tab bar to open the method picker.

The picker has a search box at the top (search by name or ), a Recently used in this project section pinned to the top, and a folder-grouped list below. Hover or arrow through entries to preview the method body in the right pane, then press Enter or click to attach.

When you open a method tab on an experiment, the amber Variation Notes bar sits at the top. Expand it and click + Add Note to add a timestamped entry where you can describe what you did differently for this run (e.g., "halved the elongation time", "substituted Q5 for Phusion")...

For PCR methods , you can also edit the thermal gradient or recipe table directly inside the experiment tab. Your edits save as an experiment-local copy when you click Save Changes , so the original protocol in the library stays untouched. A Reset to Method button reverts the ...
Markdown and PDF methods don't copy the body onto the experiment. The Variation Notes panel is the only place where per-experiment changes are recorded for those formats.

Markdown is the default. Free-form protocol text in the same markdown editor used for lab notes and results, with toolbars, image drag-drop, and live preview. See The Markdown Editor for the full shortcut set.
PDF lets you upload an existing protocol PDF (a kit insert, a published paper). PDFs render in an iframe and can't be edited inline.
PCR opens a thermal-gradient editor and reaction-recipe table. Full details on the PCR Reaction Builder page.
LC Gradient opens a solvent-gradient chart editor with flow, column, and mobile-phase fields for HPLC and LC-MS protocols.
Plate Layout renders an interactive well-plate grid with sample, control, and blank annotations.
Cell culture passaging tracks a passaging schedule, media, and cell line, with per-task passage history.
Mass spec captures ionization mode, source and scan params, and calibration. Pairs with LC for LC-MS workflows.
qPCR analysis records Cq readouts, melt-curve Tm, standard-curve efficiency, and ΔΔCq fold-change.
Coding workflow stores reusable scripts (Python, R, SQL) and Jupyter notebooks alongside protocol text.
Compound method bundles existing methods into one attachable kit (e.g. plate layout plus assay PDF). Reached by extending an existing method, not as a standalone picker tile.

Click + New Method . Pick a type from the Standard or Structured section and give it a name. The Folder (optional) field autocompletes against existing categories (the page-level headings) so methods drop into the right bucket.

Fill in the body: write markdown, upload a PDF, draw an LC gradient, lay out a plate, or build a PCR program and recipe. For markdown methods you can drag images and attachment files directly into the editor.
Click Create Method . The new card lands in its category on the Method Library page and is immediately available to attach to experiments.
The picker's search box matches names and tags (prefix with to search tags only). Many labs end up with five slightly different copies of the same protocol. A quick search prevents that.
The Method Library: cards grouped under category headings, with type pills on each card.
The Methods page showing categories of method cards, with type pills marking the method type on each card.

entries.title: Notifications & Inbox
headings: Right-click a single row to skip the active-experiment dance
The toast is your fastest path to filing
bodySnippets: The bell, the Telegram inbox, and event reminders. All three live in the app shell, so you see new things wherever you're working.
ResearchOS shows you new things in three places:
Click the bell to drop down a list of notifications. Each row is one of three kinds:

Clicking anywhere on an unread row marks it as read but doesn't go anywhere. Clicking a row that is already read is a no-op. Each row also has a blue pill with a checkmark on the right that marks just that one item as read, making it the primary affordance for silencing a sing...

At the top of the dropdown, Mark all read clears the unread badge without removing anything. It only appears when at least one unread item exists. Clear read removes the rows you've already acknowledged, and only appears when at least one read item is present. Clear all emptie...

The bell rechecks for new notifications every 30 seconds while the app is open. When something fires inside this tab (e.g., a calendar reminder), the badge updates immediately rather than waiting for the next poll. Shift alerts are different: another lab member writes them int...

The Inbox pill in the header counts photos sitting in your Telegram inbox folder. Click it to open the Inbox modal, a centered popup over the rest of the app. Each row shows a thumbnail, the caption you sent with the photo, the original filename, and the time it arrived.
Each row has these actions on the right:
Clicking a single row opens an image editor where you can rename the file, edit the caption, see when it was received, and either move it to the active experiment or delete it.
For a stack of photos that all belong to the same experiment, the modal supports multi-select so you don't have to file them one at a time:

With one or more rows selected, right-click any selected row (or click the ⋯ button) to open a context menu. The top two items read Send to task... and Send to note... for a single row, or Send N items to task... / Send N items to note... when multiple are selected. Picking ei...

If any filename in the batch collides with an existing image in the destination task, the duplicate-resolution dialog pops up for each collision so you can Rename , Replace , or Cancel per item. Non-colliding items go through without interruption. When the batch finishes, a gr...

The moment a new photo arrives in your Telegram inbox, a toast appears in the bottom-right corner with a thumbnail, the caption, and a one-click action. If an experiment popup is open, the action is File here , which moves the photo straight into that experiment. If nothing's ...

Toasts auto-dismiss after about 12 seconds, and any photo you file or delete elsewhere also drops out of the toast queue automatically. The small ✕ on a toast just dismisses the notification, the photo stays in the inbox until you act on it.
The bell handles your personal queue (shared-with-me, reminders, shift alerts). Comments and announcements live in a separate surface: the Lab Inbox . The two are intentionally distinct.

The bell does ping for one comment case: when someone @-mentions you by name in a comment, that mention shows up as a row in the bell so you do not miss a direct callout. General comments on records you can read stay in the Lab Inbox only. See Comments for the threading and me...
Reminders are a single setting that applies to every timed event on your calendar. Open the Calendar page and click Reminders at the top of the left sidebar. The popup has:

With reminders on, every timed event in the next 24 hours is queued. When the chosen lead time hits, a row appears in the bell and (if you've granted browser permission) an OS notification pops on top of whatever you're doing. All-day events don't produce reminders, since ther...

Reminder settings, the bell history, and the Telegram inbox are all per-user. If you share a folder with the rest of your lab, everyone has their own bell, their own inbox, and their own reminder preferences.

The bell in the top-right of the header collects everything someone shared with you (tasks, methods, projects), calendar reminders that have fired, and shift alerts when another lab member reschedules a shared task. A red count badge appears when there are unread items.
The Inbox pill , right next to the bell, opens a centered modal of Telegram photos waiting to be filed into an experiment. The pill turns amber and shows a count when photos are waiting.
An inbox toast appears at the bottom-right corner the moment a new Telegram photo arrives, so you can file it in one click without hunting for the modal.
Shared with you. When another lab member shares a task, method, or project with you, a row appears here showing who shared it, what it was, and whether you got view or edit access.
Event reminder. When a calendar reminder you set up fires (see below), a row lands here with the event title, the time it starts, and how many minutes you asked to be warned in advance.

Shift alert. When another lab member with edit access reschedules a task you also have access to, a row appears reading "Alex shifted PCR optimization by +3d" with the old and new start dates underneath. The amber delta covers start-date moves and, when start and end shifted b...

Move to active. Sends the photo into whichever surface is currently open in ResearchOS: an experiment, a note, or (if both popups are open) a small dropdown lets you pick between them. The button is disabled when nothing is open, with a tooltip explaining why.

⋯ (more actions). A small ellipsis button that only fades in when you hover the row. Clicking it opens the same menu as right-click, anchored under the button: Send to task... , Send to note... , Move to active , and Delete .
Delete. Removes the photo from the inbox folder (with a confirm prompt).
Shift-click a second row to select the contiguous range from your last anchor row to this one.
Cmd-click (or Ctrl-click on Windows / Linux) to toggle individual rows in and out of the selection without disturbing the rest.
Selected rows pick up a blue border and a blue ring so you can see the group at a glance. Clicking the empty area of the modal clears the selection.
The bell pings you for things you need to acknowledge: a labmate shared a task with you, a calendar reminder fired, your task was shifted by someone with edit access.
The Lab Inbox is ambient. Comments people left on your tasks and notes appear there, alongside the lab-wide announcement stream and the @-mention filter.
A master Enable reminders checkbox.
A Remind me dropdown for how far in advance to fire (1 minute, 5, 10, 15, 30, 1 hour, 2 hours, or 1 day before).
A button to grant your browser permission to show OS-level popups, plus a Send test link once it's granted.

Right-click any inbox row (no multi-select needed) and pick Send to task... to send that one photo to any experiment, even one you don't have open. It's the fastest way to file a photo into the right experiment without first opening that experiment's popup.
If a photo lands while you're working in an experiment, the toast lets you attach it in one click without opening the Inbox modal at all.

Because ResearchOS has no server, reminders only fire while a ResearchOS tab is open in your browser. If you close every tab, the in-app row still appears the next time you open the app, but no live OS popup will go off in the meantime. For phone-side or always-on-but-laptop-a...
The bell and the Inbox pill sit together in the top-right of the header, next to the help and settings icons.
The notification bell with a count badge and the Inbox pill button beside it in the top-right of the app header.
Shift-alert row in the bell. The amber delta is the headline. View task jumps to the new start date on the calendar, Ignore dismisses the row.
A shift-alert row in the bell dropdown reading 'Alex shifted PCR optimization by +3d', with old and new start dates underneath, and View task and Ignore buttons.
Open the Inbox to see waiting photos. Each row has a Move-to-active and Delete button, and clicking the row opens an editor for the caption and filename.
The Inbox modal showing waiting Telegram photos with thumbnails, captions, timestamps, and per-row Move and Delete buttons.
Shift-click or Cmd-click to select multiple inbox photos, then right-click for the batch menu. The label updates to reflect how many items will be sent.
The Inbox modal with three rows selected (blue borders) and the right-click context menu showing Send 3 items to task, Move to active, and Delete.

entries.title: PCR Protocols
headings: The four zones of a program
Build a protocol step by step
Attaching a protocol to an experiment
Checking off ingredients at the bench
bodySnippets: Save reusable PCR programs (temperature steps plus reagent mix) and attach them to experiments the same way you attach a method.
Each protocol opens in a popup with two panels stacked on top of each other:
It is the same library-plus-variation pattern as Methods . ResearchOS keeps the saved protocol in your library and copies its gradient and reagent values onto each experiment that attaches it.

Reading the gradient panel left to right, every protocol moves through four zones in this order. The default that a brand-new protocol starts with shows all four, so a new protocol is also the quickest way to see the layout.

Block color is keyed to temperature across six bands: blue (≤ 15 °C), cyan (≤ 30 °C), green (≤ 50 °C), amber (≤ 70 °C), orange (≤ 85 °C), and red (above 85 °C). A step typed in at the wrong temperature is visually obvious because its color doesn't match its neighbors.

From an experiment popup, the Attach PCR Protocol picker shows every protocol in your library, your own and any in the Shared folder. Pick one and ResearchOS copies the gradient and the reagent table onto that experiment.

From that point on, the experiment carries its own copy. Bump the annealing temperature by a degree, add another microliter of primer, knock the cycle count down to 28 for a low-template run. The experiment records the variation and the library protocol stays exactly as you le...

When you open a PCR protocol on an experiment (via the Method tab), the Reaction Recipe table gains a checkoff column on the left. Each ingredient row has a small checkbox. Tick it as you pipette each reagent into your tube. Checked rows turn green and a progress bar at the to...

Protocols are stored under your user by default and visible only to you. The API supports a public flag that would write to , but the creation UI does not expose this toggle . Sharing a PCR protocol is currently backend-only. The picker on each experiment shows protocols from ...

The Thermal Gradient panel up top: a left-to-right row of colored square blocks, one per step. Each block shows the temperature and duration of that step. A purple dashed rectangle wraps the cycled section and carries a small badge for the repeat count.

The Reaction Recipe panel below: one row per ingredient with a stock concentration and a per-reaction volume in microliters. The bottom Total row is a manual entry where you type the target reaction volume. It looks identical to the ingredient rows above it (no input border, n...
Initial steps : one or more blocks that run once before the cycled section. Typically initial denaturation around 95 °C for two minutes, painted red.

Cycled block : a group of blocks wrapped in a purple dashed rectangle, repeated end-to-end by the count on the purple badge above it (35 by default). The usual three are denaturation, annealing, and extension. Steps inside this block carry a faint purple ring so you can tell a...
Final steps : one or more blocks after the cycled block that run once. Typically final extension at 72 °C for three minutes.
Hold : a single block at the right end with the duration Indef. , parking the thermocycler at a low temperature (12 °C by default) until you come back to it. Painted blue.
Click + New Protocol in the top-right of the PCR page. Type a name into the Protocol Name field at the top of the popup.

The gradient panel opens with a sensible default loaded in (initial denaturation, the three-step cycle at 35 repeats, final extension, and a hold). Click the Edit Cycle button on the toolbar to enter editing mode. The blocks start jiggling to signal that they are now interactive.

Edit any block. Double-click a block to open the Edit Step popup. It has three fields: Step Name (e.g. Annealing ), Temperature (°C) as a number input, and Duration as free text (e.g. 20 sec , 2 min ). The Hold checkbox next to the duration field locks duration to Indef. for t...

Reorder. Single-click a block to select it (a blue ring appears) and a pair of ← → arrow buttons drop in above the block. The arrows swap the block with its neighbor. The arrows step around the cycled block rather than into it: a block outside the cycle stays outside, and a bl...

Move a step in or out of the cycle. When a block inside the cycled rectangle is selected, a red Remove from Cycle button appears next to the arrows. When a block outside the cycle is selected, a purple Add to Cycle button appears with a dropdown listing each cycle by position ...
Adjust the repeat count. Click the purple badge above the cycled rectangle to open the Edit Cycle Repeats popup. Type a new number (1–100) and save.

Add or remove blocks. The toolbar has + Add Step (opens a StepEditPopup pre-populated with "New Step", 60 °C, 30 sec; the block only lands in the gradient after you click Save ) and + Add Cycle (opens an Add Empty Cycle confirmation modal with Cancel and Add buttons; the new e...
Hit ✓ Done Editing to leave editing mode. The jiggle stops and the toolbar collapses.

Scroll down to Reaction Recipe . Fill in each row with a stock concentration (e.g. 10x ) and a per-reaction volume in microliters. Use + Add Row at the bottom of the table to add ingredients, and the x on each row to remove one. The Total row at the bottom is a manual entry. T...

Type any free-text Notes (lot numbers, master mix quirks, the day's primer pair). Click Create Protocol . The card appears in your protocol library and shows up on the Attach PCR Protocol picker in any experiment.

Same model as Methods. When a PCR protocol is attached to an experiment, any edits to volumes, temperatures, or cycle counts apply only to that run. The library copy stays untouched for the next person who attaches it.
The protocol editor: thermal gradient on top, reagent table below.
The PCR protocol editor with a row of colored temperature blocks on top and a reagent table below.
Double-click any block to open the Edit Step popup.
The Edit Step popup with fields for step name, temperature, and duration, plus a Hold checkbox.
The recipe table sits below the gradient. The Total row at the bottom is a manual entry that holds your target reaction volume.
The Reaction Recipe table with rows for each ingredient and a checkoff checkbox column, plus a Total row at the bottom.

entries.title: Project Surface
bodySnippets: Each project has two faces. The Home page card for quick stats and card-level actions, and a full route page that hosts the project's hypothesis prose, results, methods, goals, and activity.
Each project is represented by a card on the Home page grid. From top to bottom, a card contains:
Clicking anywhere on the card (other than a task name or the kebab) navigates directly to the project's Workspace route at . There is no popup intermediate step.
Archived projects do not appear in the active grid. They show in a separate Archived Projects section below the active grid, rendered as muted cards with an archived-date badge.
Hover any project card to reveal a three-dots kebab button in the top-right corner. Clicking it opens a dropdown with the following items:

The Workspace is a single scrolling page with a sticky top bar. The top bar carries the project's color stripe, a ← Projects breadcrumb back to Home, the project name, and icon buttons for Edit , Share , Archive , and Delete . A View timeline -> link jumps you across to the Ga...

Below the icons, a row of anchor links lets you jump between sections without scrolling: Overview │ Results │ Methods │ Activity . The anchor strip stays pinned as you scroll, so the jump links are always within reach.
The Overview is where you write the project's hypothesis, motivation, and big-picture context. It is a plain resizable textarea with no toolbar, no image-paste, and no drag-drop file attachment.

Edits autosave 1.5 seconds after the last keystroke. A small Saving... / Saved indicator near the section title tells you when the write has landed. The text lives in a sidecar file at , so it is portable, greppable, and editable outside the app.

The Results section pulls every image from every Results tab on every experiment that belongs to this project, then groups them by experiment. Each group has a collapsible header with the experiment name and the image count. Within a group, thumbnails are sorted newest-first. ...

Experiments hosted into this project by labmates (their experiments, attached to your project) appear in their own groups with a Shared by <owner> chip. Hosted groups are suppressed on archived projects.

The Methods section is a flat, deduplicated inventory of every method attached to an experiment in this project. Each row carries the method name, a type pill (Markdown, PDF, PCR), and a used in N experiments badge so you can see which protocols this project actually leans on.

Rows are sorted by usage count descending, with the method name as an alphabetical tiebreaker. Click any row to jump to the full method in the Method Library . If the same method appears on hosted experiments (from labmates), you'll see a via <owner> chip next to the row.

If you turned Goals on during the Welcome Wizard , a fourth section slots in between Methods and Activity. It lists every high-level goal whose matches this project, with active goals on top and completed ones below. Each row shows the goal's color dot, name, date range, and S...

Click a row to open the goal editor. The section heading reads Goals with a sibling count badge to its right showing the total number of goals on the project. If you skipped Goals during the wizard, the section is hidden entirely (no empty placeholder, no marketing CTA). You c...

Activity is a newest-first feed of events scoped to this project. The feed picks up task completions, image drops on any of the project's experiments, methods attached or removed, overview edits, share events, and archive flips. Each row carries an icon, a one-line summary, an...
Events live in a sidecar at , which means every collaborator on a shared project sees the same feed. Events older than 90 days are lazily pruned the next time the file is written.
When a labmate shares a project with you, the URL gains an query parameter so the Workspace knows which user's files to read.
How much you can change depends on the permission you were granted:
On the Workspace, a small Shared by <owner> chip sits next to the project name so you always know whose namespace you're reading from.
ResearchOS ships with a built-in Miscellaneous project for standalone tasks that don't belong to a specific research project. It is treated differently on every Project Surface:
Color stripe across the top edge, matching the project's chosen color.
Project name , with an optional 7-day badge when weekends are active on that project.
Progress bar showing completed tasks out of total, with a percentage.
Active / Overdue / Upcoming stats row.
Next Up task list (up to 5 upcoming tasks). Clicking a task name here opens that task's detail popup directly.
Tag chips at the bottom for any tags attached to the project.
Drag handle (own cards only). Own project cards show a six-dot handle on the right for reordering. Cards for projects shared to you have no handle and cannot be reordered.
Kebab menu (own and edit-permission cards). A three-dots button appears in the top-right corner on hover (see "Card actions" below).
Edit opens the EditProjectModal, where you can rename the project, change its color, edit tags, and toggle the 7-day schedule. Disabled (cursor-not-allowed) for view-only receivers.
Share opens the share dialog so you can grant a labmate access. This item is hidden entirely on projects that were shared to you. Only the original owner can grant access.

Archive / Unarchive triggers an amber confirmation dialog before archiving. Archived projects keep all their tasks but move to the separate Archived Projects section below the active grid. Their tasks stop showing in the Gantt and the task sidebar. Unarchive from the same keba...
Delete is disabled (grayed out) for any receiver, view or edit permission. Only the original owner can destroy the project file.

View permission. In the kebab menu and the Workspace top bar, Edit and Archive render disabled (cursor-not-allowed). The Share item is omitted entirely from the kebab (not just disabled) for any shared-in card. The Workspace still loads, and you can read the overview, browse t...

Edit permission. The Edit and Archive actions are live in both the kebab and the Workspace top bar. Your writes route back to the owner's directory, so they reflect on their copy too. The Delete action is still owner-only: only the original owner can destroy the project file, ...
The kebab menu is suppressed entirely for Miscellaneous cards. Edit, Share, Archive, and Delete are not available from the Home grid.

Clicking the card navigates to the project route at , where you can see progress, the next-up task list, and the activity feed. The route top bar also hides Edit, Share, Archive, and Delete for Miscellaneous.
There is no Overview, Results, or Methods section because Miscellaneous has no hypothesis, no experiments, and no protocol inventory worth deduplicating.
From the Home grid, hover a project card to reveal the three-dots kebab in the top-right corner. Use it for project-level CRUD (rename, share, archive, delete).
Click anywhere else on the card (or a task name in the Next Up list to open that task directly) to navigate to the Workspace route.
On the Workspace, write into the Overview textarea, scroll for Results, Methods, optional Goals, and Activity. Use the anchor strip to jump between sections.
Use the ← Projects breadcrumb in the top bar to return to the Home grid.
Your own projects live at . A project a labmate has shared with you appends an owner hint: . That second segment is how ResearchOS picks the right per-user file path when ids collide across labmates.

A built-in project called Miscellaneous holds standalone tasks that don't belong to a research project. The kebab menu is suppressed entirely for Miscellaneous. Clicking the card navigates to the project route, which shows progress, next-up tasks, and activity, but Edit, Share...

Goals are a deliberate workflow, not a default. The wizard's fourth question asks whether you want to commit to multi-week outcomes. If you answered no, the rest of the app respects that and the project route doesn't litter the anchor strip with a feature you opted out of.
Miscellaneous is a permanent catch-all for ad-hoc tasks. The card is non-actionable from the kebab by design. The project route still works as a read-only progress view.

Project Workspaces are first-class URLs. Bookmark them, paste them into your lab notebook, or drop the link into a Slack thread. Anyone with view-or-edit access on a shared project lands on the same view when they open the URL.
The Workspace at /workbench/projects/1, scrolled to Overview.
The project route page with a color stripe, breadcrumb back to Projects, the project name, and a sticky anchor strip reading Overview Results Methods Activity.
Results are grouped by experiment, newest images first.
The Results section of the project route, showing two experiment groups each with their own header row and a strip of thumbnail images.
The Methods inventory deduplicates across experiments and counts usage.
The Methods section of the project route, listing several methods with type pills and a 'used in N experiments' badge on each row.
Activity is a chronological feed scoped to this project.
The Activity section, listing a chronological feed of events. Each row has an icon, a summary line, and a relative timestamp on the right.

entries.title: Purchases & Funding
headings: Cost transparency, not a filing cabinet
Vendor and category as first-class fields
The loose model and the soft warning
Breakdown by project, vendor, or category
bodySnippets: The Purchases page answers a single question for grant management: where is the money going right now? It pairs a flat reverse-chronological list of every purchase order with a live spending dashboard, so a PI can scan recent activity and check budget burn without leaving the ...

A purchase order in ResearchOS is a task with its task_type set to . Each row on the page is one of those tasks, and inside each row is a small spreadsheet of line items (the actual things you bought). The page is built around lookup, not data entry. Lab purchase lifecycles ar...

The header at the top reads Purchases · N orders · $X.XX total , where the total comes from every line item across every order visible to you. To the right is the Manage Funding Accounts button, which expands a panel inline above the list so you can add or edit g...

The amber + New Purchase button in the top-right of the page header opens . The modal collapses the two-step data model (parent purchase task + first line item) into a single form, so the common case of logging one item takes one interaction instead of two.
The modal has six fields:

On save, the modal creates a parent task dated today with a one-day duration, then immediately creates the first line item under it. After saving you can expand the row in the list and use the inline PurchaseEditor to add more line items to the same order.

Above the order list is a three-chip segmented control that scopes what you see. All shows every purchase task and is always visible. Project purchases filters to orders attached to a real project, hiding the Miscellaneous bucket. The third chip, Miscellaneous , shows only the...

The list itself is a single column sorted by start date, newest first. There is no active vs earlier split. A completed order looks almost identical to an in-flight one: same row, same colors, with a small green dot and the text appended to the metadata line. The row itself is...

Click any row to expand it into a line-item table. Each row is one thing you are buying with columns for item name, quantity, vendor link, CAS or accession number, price per unit, shipping, computed total, the funding account paying for it, plus the new Vendor and Category col...

The existing data model requires every purchase task to have a . Purchases that do not belong to any real project, like conference flights, lab snacks, or one-off equipment, still need somewhere to land. Rather than making nullable and updating every reader (Gantt, Workbench, ...

The hidden project never appears on Home, Workbench, Gantt, the project picker, or anywhere in the app that calls without explicitly opting in. The route is the only surface that passes , so the bucket is visible (and filterable) there but invisible everywhere else.

Lab purchasing has heavy vendor reuse. The same supplier shows up across dozens of orders, often spelled three different ways. To make that data useful for analytics, every line item now carries a vendor and a category , both stored as nullable free-text strings on . They are ...

Each input is wired to a sourced from , deduplicated, non-null values only. So the first time you type into a vendor cell, the dropdown surfaces every distinct vendor you (or anyone sharing with you) has typed before. Suggestions update as new values are added elsewhere, inclu...

ResearchOS does not block you from attaching a purchase item to a task whose type is not . That is on purpose. Reagents legitimately get tied to a specific experiment task, and forcing a rigid type boundary would push researchers into either making every-experiment-also-a-purc...

Opening a purchase editor against a non-purchase task surfaces a soft amber note above the line-item table. The system does not block you. The items will show up under Items on non-purchase tasks in the dashboard below so they stay visible.

Scroll past the order list and the page closes out with a live spending dashboard. Every number on it is recomputed from the same set of items you can see above. The on-disk field is known-stale and is not used here. Live computation avoids drift bugs when an item is edited or...

The top of the dashboard has two controls. The time range dropdown chooses the window: Last 30 days , Last 90 days , Last 12 months (the default), All time , or a Custom date range with from and to inputs. The All projects checkbox lets the dashboard ignore the global project ...

Below the controls is a row of cards, one per funding account. Each card shows the account name, dollars spent, total budget, dollars remaining, and a progress bar. The numbers are computed live from items in the current window. Over-budget cards turn red. Items with a funding...

Next is a vertical bar chart of monthly spend across the selected window. Empty months still render as zero-height bars so the time span on the x-axis matches your range selection. Hover any bar to see the dollar total and item count for that month.

Below the time chart is a horizontal bar chart with a segmented control in the section header to switch lenses. The same set of items in the current window is regrouped by project, by vendor, or by category. Items with no value for the active lens (no vendor set, no category s...

Near the bottom of the dashboard is a thin amber line that reads Items on non-purchase tasks: followed by a count and a dollar total. Click it to expand an inline table of those items with their host task name and amount. This panel exists because the loose model lets you legi...

The top-right of the dashboard has an Export CSV button that downloads the currently filtered scope (time range plus project filter, plus any funding-card filter you have clicked) as a flat file named . Columns are item id, item name, vendor, category, funding string, project ...

Next to the export button, PIs see a Open in Lab Overview shortcut that opens the same purchase data inside the LabPurchases Tool popup on /lab-overview , where the funding-bar, burn-rate, and pending-approval surfaces all live in one place.

PIs do not have in their nav. The canonical surface for them is the LabPurchases Tool inside the Lab Overview, opened either from a pinned widget tile or from the Tools launcher in the header. The popup is a four-tab dashboard:

A declined purchase carries a timestamp and renders with a red wherever it appears: in the member's own list, in the All Purchases tab, in the Lab Activity stream. The Pending Approvals tab also shows a Recently declined section at the bottom so a PI can Re-approve a previousl...
On the Lab Overview, the purchases Tool has three tile variants you can pin to the canvas or the sidebar rail:
Clicking any of the three variant tiles opens the same four-tab popup described above. See Widgets and Tools for the broader catalog.

Click Manage Funding Accounts at the top right and a panel opens inline above the order list. The button label flips to Hide Funding Manager while it is open, so you can collapse it back down without leaving the page. Each account has a name (for example ), a total budget, and...

The dashboard uses Recharts ( in ), lazy-loaded on the route. It is code-split out of the shared root bundle, so adding the charts here did not slow down the rest of the app. Around 104 KB gzipped on this one route.
A few things in this area are not yet shipped, listed here so you do not spend time looking for them:

Item Name (required): a native autocomplete surfaces every distinct item name from your purchase history, de-duped case-insensitively with the most-recent record winning. When the typed value matches a prior item name exactly, Vendor and Price per unit fill in automatically. Q...
Vendor : free text, optional. Not auto-filled unless an exact Item Name match triggers the recall logic above.

Category : a listing your non-archived, non-shared owned projects plus a synthetic Miscellaneous option at the bottom. Defaults to the first owned project alphabetically; falls back to Miscellaneous if you have no owned projects yet. Choosing Miscellaneous routes the purchase ...
Price per unit and Quantity : numeric free-text inputs. Price defaults blank; quantity defaults to 1.

Funding string : a autocomplete pulls from your existing funding accounts (for example, ). Typing a new string and saving creates a budget-zero funding account automatically so the string appears in future dropdowns without a separate setup step. The field is optional; leave i...

Pending approvals . The work queue. Each row is a purchase waiting on the PI with inline Approve and Decline buttons. The first action of a fresh session unlocks the 5-minute edit window; subsequent rows process without re-prompting. See Edit session and password .
All purchases . A flat lab-wide view of every purchase across every member, filterable by member, project, and funding string.
Funding . The funding-account cards (spent vs. budget) rolled up across the whole lab, not just the PI's own purchases.
Spending . The full spend-over-time chart and the breakdown lenses (project / vendor / category), same shape as the dashboard at the bottom of but with lab-wide scope.
Funding bars. A horizontal bar per funding account showing spent vs. budget. Good for "how much grant is left."
Burn rate. A small trend chart of weekly spend with a range selector. The range buttons are 4w , 8w , 12w , and 6mo . Good for "are we accelerating."
Pending count. A single big number plus the top few waiting purchases. Good for "do I need to approve something right now."
Per-item dates (the time axis uses the parent task's start date)
A timestamp
Multi-currency support
Recurring or subscription purchases
OCR receipt import
Anomaly detection and spending alerts
Budget-threshold notifications
A catalog-prefilled vendor and category list (datalist autocomplete is the only source today)

During the onboarding tour, a component mounts on the route to show Alex's fixture data as a read-only overlay. The tour also drives this modal end-to-end via the BeakerBot cursor script. Production users who are not in the tour never see the demo overlay.

Be careful not to conflate two distinct concepts that share the same label. The Category select in NewPurchaseModal picks which project the parent purchase task routes to: choosing "Miscellaneous" routes to the hidden project. Separately, the Miscellaneous filter chip on the o...

This applies to the free-text vendor and category columns on individual rows inside PurchaseEditor, not to the top-level project routing. A free-text field with autocomplete keeps the surface low-friction for researchers who already know what vendor they mean, while still lett...

When you have no purchases yet, the spend-over-time chart shows the prompt Add your first purchase to see spend breakdowns here. The funding cards still render with a zero spent value against each account's budget, so the budget scaffold is visible from day one.

Funding accounts are stored in the shared lab folder, not in your own user folder. Adding one makes it pickable on every labmate's Purchases page and in the funding dropdown when they add a line item. That is the point. One person in the lab can manage the list of grants and e...
Header, filter chips, and the unified order list. The amber '+ New Purchase' button and the segmented filter chips were added in the 2026-05-22 Purchases redesign.

The Purchases page header with an amber '+ New Purchase' button at the top right, the three filter chips 'All / Project purchases / Miscellaneous' below the header, and a single reverse-chronological list of purchase orders beneath.
An expanded order. Vendor and category are first-class columns, and both inputs draw from a datalist of values used elsewhere in your purchase history.
One purchase order expanded into a line-item table. The vendor and category columns are visible, and an autocomplete datalist suggestion is open beneath one of the vendor inputs.
The soft amber note in PurchaseEditor when the parent task is not a purchase. The Lab Overview surface does not show this warning, since the lab-wide review wants a quieter dashboard.

entries.title: Where results live now
headings: Per-project completed work → Workbench Results tab
bodySnippets: ResearchOS used to have a standalone Results page that listed every completed task, grouped by project. That page was retired. Completed work now surfaces in three places, each closer to the context where the work happens.

Experiments that you've marked complete now collect in the Earlier results section at the bottom of the Workbench page, with the header rendered in uppercase as EARLIER RESULTS . They keep all the same write-up affordances: clicking a card opens the task detail popup with the ...
Finished purchase orders appear inline in the Purchases chronological list alongside active orders, marked with a green dot and a text suffix. There is no separate Earlier section.

Clicking a project card on the Home page navigates to the project route at . The Results tab there shows a gallery of result images for that project via the component. This is the fastest way to review all result images for a single project without leaving its context.

Visiting now redirects to Workbench , where the bulk of the old page's content lives. No data moved on disk: every task lives at . After the hybrid editor, new file attachments land in per-tab subdirectories: notes files go to and results files go to , rather than a flat folde...
is queued for recapture to reflect the current Workbench layout.

The standalone Results page was a fourth surface that mostly duplicated work the per-feature pages were already doing better. Folding completion into Workbench, Purchases, and the project popup keeps each completion view closer to the live work it belongs to, instead of forcin...
The Earlier results section sits at the bottom of the Workbench. The project-grouping toggle in its header flips between flat and project-grouped views.
The Earlier results section at the bottom of the Workbench page, with completed experiment cards grouped by project.

entries.title: Search
headings: What gets searched, and what doesn't
bodySnippets: A filter-driven search page that finds tasks across every project under your user. Fill in the form, click Search, click a result card to open its task popup.

The page opens with an empty filter card and a placeholder that reads "Enter search criteria above." Type into the Keywords box (or use any of the dropdowns), then click the blue Search button. Results render below as a grid of cards, one per matching task. The Clear button ne...
Every filter is optional. Leave them blank to match everything, or combine as many as you want. All filters AND together.

Each card carries a colored stripe matching its project, the task name, a project pill, a task-type pill (purple for experiments, amber for purchases, gray for list tasks), the schedule and duration, the method (when one is attached), and the first few tags. A green Complete b...

The Search page doubles as the launch pad for bulk exports. Click the Select button next to the result count and the cards sprout a checkbox in the top-right corner. Tick one or more result cards (a blue ring and the checked mark show selection), and the header swaps in a coun...

Clicking Export selected opens the same Export dialog the experiment popup uses, with three format cards: PDF report , HTML report , and Raw ResearchOS format . The dialog heading reads Export N experiments with a reminder that multi-experiment exports produce a zip with one f...
See Export an experiment on the Experiments page for a deeper walkthrough of what each format bundles and which one to pick.
Keywords matches the task name, its tags, and the name and tags of its first attached method. Multiple words AND together (e.g., only returns tasks where both words appear).
Date From and Date To restrict to tasks whose schedule overlaps the range.
Task Type narrows to one of the three task shapes (i.e., Experiments, Purchases, or List Tasks).
Project limits results to tasks inside one project.
Specific Method matches tasks whose first attached method is the one you pick. Method Category is the broader version: it matches on the method's folder.
Completion Status filters to complete or incomplete tasks.
PDF packages each experiment as its own selectable-text PDF with table of contents and outline pane, then drops every PDF into a single .
HTML gives each experiment its own subfolder inside the zip (one self-contained HTML page plus an tree), so you can open each report independently without an extra unzip step.
Raw nests one per experiment inside the outer zip, keeping each bundle ready to drop into another ResearchOS user's Settings → Import experiment dialog.
Pressing Enter while focused on the Keywords box runs the search without needing to click the button.

Keywords match against task names, task tags, and method names and tags; they do not match against note bodies, results text, or PCR protocol contents. Open the Notes page or a method's own editor to search the inside of those files.
The Search page. Type into Keywords, narrow with the filters, then click Search.
The Search page with a keywords input, a row of filter dropdowns, and Search and Clear buttons.
Select mode on Search. Tick the cards you want and click Export selected to open the format dialog.
Search results in select mode: three cards ticked with blue rings and the Export selected button visible at the top of the results list.

entries.title: Settings
headings: What a password protects, and what it doesn't
bodySnippets: Fourteen panels stacked top to bottom: Profile, Tabs, LabArchives, AI Helper, Sidebar, View defaults, Animation, Notifications & behavior, Streaks, Data inventory, Data maintenance, Onboarding, Security, and Offline mode. Reach the page through the gear icon in the top-right o...

Each change saves the moment you toggle, type, or pick a new option. A small "Saving..." pill near the page title flips to a green "Saved" for about a second, then disappears. The path printed under the page title shows where your preferences are stored on disk ( ), which is h...

At the very top of the page, above every panel, a search bar filters the visible settings down to whatever matches your typed substring. Type and the page collapses to the Security panel; type and it narrows to the Animation section. The filter matches section headings and the...
