PI accounts ( ) see a two-tab strip at the top of the page: Personal (the default, every panel documented below) and Lab Mode (the lab-wide configuration that only a PI can change). The Personal tab is identical to what every account sees. The Lab Mode tab holds the LabRoster ...

The top panel sets how the rest of the app addresses you. A large avatar bubble on the left previews the gradient that'll appear in comments, lab views, and the user-picker. The Display name field overrides your folder name (leave it blank to fall back to the folder name).

The Primary color row is a fixed 10-color palette. Clicking a swatch updates the avatar preview instantly and refreshes every avatar bubble across the app on the next paint. A swatch is grayed out when another lab member already holds that color as a solid (direction-insensiti...

Below the primary row sits an Optional second color for gradient row. Pick any swatch that is not your primary and is not already taken by a lab-mate to create a two-stop gradient. The avatar preview updates immediately. A Clear secondary button appears next to the row label w...
The Tint header with my color toggle controls one thing: whether the top header bar uses your gradient or stays plain white. Your avatar bubbles keep your color either way.

A two-column grid of checkboxes for every tab in the header (Home, Workbench, GANTT, Methods, Purchases, Calendar, Search, Lab Links). Uncheck a tab to hide it from your header. Home is grayed out with an "always on" tag so you can't accidentally hide every tab at once.

Below the grid, the Default landing tab dropdown picks where ResearchOS opens when you load the app. The dropdown only lists tabs you have visible (plus Home), so you can't land somewhere you've hidden.

A single option card for bulk-importing a LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP into ResearchOS. The card has a title, a short description, and a small button that expands an explainer about why the existing users can opt back in here any time.
Clicking Re-run tour clears the tour's completion and skip flags so Phase 1 setup runs again, then launches the in-product walkthrough immediately without a page reload.

If a previous tour left demo data on your real account (because the auto-cleanup at the end was skipped), an amber recovery banner appears above the button. It shows the count of leftover demo items and explains that re-running the tour will offer to clean them up at the end.

A single panel for managing the password gate on this account. The line of text in the panel shows whether the password is currently set (green) or not set (gray). The Set password or Change password button opens the same Account password popup that the lock icon on the user-p...

In the popup you can set a new password (minimum 4 characters), change an existing one (current password required), or remove the password entirely. Passwords are hashed with PBKDF2-SHA-256 at 600k iterations before being written to disk.

A single toggle, Block calls to our server , that disables the two proxy routes the app uses for external data fetching: (ICS calendar sync) and (Telegram CDN file downloads). Enabling this makes the app make no outbound calls to its own server.

Direct Telegram polling (browser to ) continues regardless of this setting, because that path does not go through the app's proxy. An amber notice appears while the toggle is on, confirming which features are paused.
Settings doesn't appear in the tabs grid. It sits behind the gear icon in the header so it can never be hidden. Even if you uncheck every other tab, the gear icon and Home keep working.

The password gate stops a lab member from accidentally signing into the wrong account through the app UI and editing someone else's notes. It does not encrypt your files. Anyone with access to the shared folder (OneDrive, Dropbox, iCloud) can still read your raw markdown and i...

Since ResearchOS has no server, there's no "reset link" email. To clear a forgotten password, open the shared data folder in Finder or Explorer, go into , and delete . Sign in again and the gate is gone. A lab admin (or anyone with access to the folder) can do this for you. Th...

Every user's preferences are stored at inside the connected data folder. Switching users in the picker loads that user's settings. Switching to a different folder gives you a clean slate of defaults until you tweak it.

The Settings page showing its stack of panels: Profile, Tabs, LabArchives, AI Helper, Sidebar, View defaults, Animation, Notifications & behavior, Streaks, Data inventory, Data maintenance, Onboarding, Security, and Offline mode.

entries.title: Sharing and permissions
headings: The two primitives: canRead and canWrite
Older records with permission: view | edit
bodySnippets: Every shareable record in ResearchOS (a method, a note, a task, a project, a high-level goal) carries one sharing field: shared_with, an array of small objects pairing a username with a permission level. That field plus a single sentinel value for 'the whole lab' is the entire...
Every shareable record carries a field whose TypeScript signature is:

Each entry pairs a recipient with the level of access they get on this record. An empty array means "private to me": the owner is implicit and never appears in their own list. Adding an entry grants that user the chosen level. Removing the entry revokes access entirely. The sh...
Inspecting your JSON folder, you will see the objects on disk. A method shared with two members at different levels looks like:

A reserved username, the constant with the value , can appear in any entry where a real username would. It means "every user in this lab folder, present and future." When you click Share with All Lab Users in any share dialog, the affordance writes one entry, (or for whole-lab...

The sentinel is comparable to a Unix group: one entry that expands to the current member set at read time. The backing list is not stored, so adding or archiving a lab member never requires a sweep across every record to keep things in sync.
Every permission decision in the app routes through one of two pure, synchronous functions:
Both functions are pure: same input, same output, no I/O. They are synchronous, so every render and every save can call them without async ceremony.

A user whose gets an extra rule on the read side only: returns true for every record in the lab regardless of . The Lab Overview cross-lab dashboards depend on this rule: the member workload widget has to be able to read every member's active tasks even when those tasks are pr...

The implicit view-all does not extend to writes. A PI reading a member's private note can leave a comment (comments respect read access) but cannot edit the note body unless the lab-head edit-session is unlocked for that member's data. See PI for the broader role and edit sess...

One non-obvious rule sits next to : when a user shares a task with you, you also get transient read access on any method that task references. The pure helper in performs the depth-1 check, and the read path emits a entry into the method owner's audit log so they can see who h...
Sharing is per-record:
Pages elsewhere in the wiki that touch sharing decisions:
canRead(record, viewer) : true if the viewer is the owner, OR the viewer's account_type is (implicit view-all), OR has an entry whose matches the viewer OR is .

canWrite(record, viewer, session) : true if the viewer is the owner, OR the viewer is a PI AND the edit-session is currently unlocked for the record's owner (the Phase 5 passcode flow, see PI edit session ), OR has an entry whose matches (or is ) AND the entry's is .

Methods can be shared with the whole lab or with specific users. Public-sharing a method makes it readable across libraries; the structured-method editor still gates writes on , which usually keeps edits to the original creator. See Methods Library .

Projects share the project itself plus every task under it. The dialog offers view-only or edit permission. The owner's Home page is the source of truth; the receiver sees a live read of the owner's data.
Tasks can be shared one at a time when you want a single experiment visible without sharing the whole project.
Notes can be shared the same way.
Methods Library covers method-level sharing UI.
Home and Projects covers project-level sharing and the receiver's view.
The Workbench covers task-level sharing.
PI covers the implicit view-all rule.
Security covers what the gate actually protects against (and what it does not).

Pre-R1 records (and older share-API callers) used a field with values or instead of . The reader ( in ) accepts both shapes so the rest of the app only ever sees the unified field ( maps to ; unknown values fall back to , the conservative default). Records still on the legacy ...

ResearchOS used to ship with a special "Lab Mode" account that held shared records on behalf of the whole lab. That mode has been retired in favor of per-user accounts plus the sharing primitive described above; pre-retirement folders auto-migrate on first login. Any old recor...
The share dialog. Add explicit usernames at a read or edit level, or pick All Lab Users to set the whole-lab sentinel.
A share dialog over a method popup, showing a list of usernames already in shared_with plus the All Lab Users sentinel option.

entries.title: Trash & History
headings: What R2 does not yet do
bodySnippets: A recovery window for deletes. When you delete a note, task, project, method, purchase, goal, lab link, or mass spec protocol, it does not vanish. It moves into a per-user trash folder where it sits for 30 days. Within that window you can restore it back to its original locati...

Research data has a different relationship to time than consumer data. A note about a PCR run from eight months ago is not "old, who cares," it is the experimental record. A misclick on the delete button used to be irrecoverable; the new trash flow gives you a safety net witho...

When you delete a note, the file moves from into . A small metadata block is added to the trashed record that records when the delete happened, who issued it, and when the auto-cleanup sweep will permanently remove it.

A sidecar index at keeps a flat summary of every trashed record. The index is rebuilt automatically from the directory listing if it ever goes out of sync (manual file deletion, OneDrive merge conflict, partial crash). The on-disk files are the ground truth; the index is just ...
Open the trash from the small trash-can icon in the top-right corner of the header (next to the Settings gear) or from the Settings -> History & Trash section. Each row shows:
A sort dropdown in the header switches between Newest first (default), Oldest first, and Expiring soon (urgent-cleanup-first).

Defaults to 30 days . Change it under Settings -> History & Trash . Four options: 7 days, 30 days, 90 days, or Never. The Never option means automatic cleanup never fires; you can still delete from the trash page manually.

The cleanup pass runs once on every folder-connect. If you keep ResearchOS open for weeks at a time, the sweep waits until your next cold start to clear expired entries. Multi-device users get the same behavior on each device when they connect their folder.
Only the record owner sees the Delete button. If a labmate has shared edit access to one of your notes, they can edit it, but they cannot delete it; that's your call as the owner.

The one carve-out is the PI lab-head edit session. When a PI is in an active Phase 5 unlock , they can delete records owned by other lab members. The trash entry records the PI as the deleter and ties the entry to the unlock session so the audit log groups consistently with th...
Click Restore on the row. The record is written back to its original path with the metadata block stripped off, and the trash file is removed. The record is now live again at the same id.
When restoring a record whose parent (e.g. a Project that contains the task) is also in trash, a prompt asks whether to restore both or just the child. See "Restoring with a trashed parent" below.

Use Permanent delete on the row to remove a trash entry ahead of the cleanup window. The button asks for confirmation and is final. After permanent delete, the record is gone; there is no second-level recycle bin.
Projects are the one entity type with TWO recovery states. Both coexist by design:

Records like Tasks (parent: Project), Notes (no parent), Purchase Items (parent: Task), and High-level Goals (parent: Project) record a soft reference to their parent at delete time. When you restore a record whose parent is ALSO in trash, a prompt asks:
If the parent is not in trash (the common case), no prompt fires and the restore proceeds straight through.

Edit history (a per-record audit log + revert button on every popup) lands later in R4 / R5 / R6. The settings panel also surfaces an "Orphaned files" row as a placeholder. Image attachments referenced only by deleted notes stay on disk for now; a cleanup tool that finds and r...
The record's name (recovered from the original title).
A small badge for the entity type.
Who deleted it and when.
A countdown until auto-cleanup ("Expires in 27 days").
A Restore button (returns the record to its original location) and a Permanent delete button (removes the trash file immediately, with a confirmation step).

Archive (the existing button): the project stays at with . It is hidden from default views but visible in the archived list. Use this for projects you are done with but want to keep on disk for reference.

Delete (now routes through trash): the project file moves to and the cleanup window starts. The trash entry preserves the flag, so a project you archived first and then deleted will come back archived on restore.
Restore both (default): restores the parent first, then this record.
Just this record : leaves the parent in trash. The record will exist but its parent reference will dangle.
Cancel : nothing happens.
Each user's cleanup window applies to their own trash. If a PI deletes one of your records during an unlock session, the trash entry lands in your folder and inherits your cleanup setting.
You can archive a project today and trash it next month. Restoring from trash strips the trash metadata only; the archive state survives the round trip.

Every delete you make through the app is recoverable for at least seven days (the most aggressive cleanup setting). Within the default 30-day window, a misclick is a one-button fix. We do not promise the trash is tamper-proof; the files sit in your folder and you can edit them...

entries.title: Getting Started
bodySnippets: Short pages that take you from a brand-new visitor to a working ResearchOS install, or skip ahead to the in-browser demo.
Work through these in order:

entries.title: Browser Requirements
headings: Keep Firefox or Safari as your daily driver
bodySnippets: ResearchOS reads and writes files directly on your computer, which only some browsers can do.
Google Chrome (version 86+) on Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS.
Microsoft Edge (version 86+) on Windows, macOS.
Brave on Windows, macOS, Linux.
Other Chromium-based browsers (e.g., Arc, Vivaldi, Opera) usually work but aren't officially tested.
Firefox doesn't implement the File System Access API. Your data is still stored in inside your folder, so once Firefox ships support, your existing data will load.
Safari (macOS / iOS) has the same limitation as Firefox.
Mobile browsers (e.g., Chrome / Edge / Brave on iOS or Android) won't work. The API is desktop-only.
Chrome : google.com/chrome
Edge : microsoft.com/edge (preinstalled on Windows 10/11)
Brave : brave.com

ResearchOS uses the File System Access API to read and write JSON files directly to a folder you pick. This is what lets the app run with no server, no account, and no upload of your data. While Firefox and Safari haven't shipped support yet, those browsers can't open Research...
You only need a Chromium browser open while you're using ResearchOS . Many users keep Firefox or Safari for everyday browsing and open Chrome only for ResearchOS.

entries.title: Connecting Your Folder
headings: The page layout, at a glance
What gets created inside the folder
Setting up a shared lab folder
The walkthrough is optional and doesn't link a folder
Already have data? Pick the same folder.
bodySnippets: Pick a folder on your disk. ResearchOS will read and write JSON files inside it (no cloud, no upload). On a brand-new visit, BeakerBot waves at you from the upper-right with an optional 3-minute walkthrough you can take before linking anything.
Three things are on this screen on a first visit:

The Take the 3-minute walkthrough button on the picker opens a small 4-beat modal that introduces the app before you commit to picking a folder. It is opt-in, not auto-fire: brand-new users see the speech bubble's gentle nudge ("New here? It is strongly recommended to take a s...

A small Skip link sits in the corner of every beat so you can bail back to the picker at any time. The modal does NOT write anything to disk and does NOT persist a "seen" flag; reopening it is a one-click decision you make each visit.
ResearchOS creates a simple tree the first time you connect:
Everything is plain JSON and plain image files. You can back up the folder by copying it, version-control it with git, or open it in Finder / Explorer at any time.

After your first connect, ResearchOS remembers the folder name via browser storage. On your next visit, instead of the two-card picker you see a dedicated reconnect screen : it shows the folder name in a bold heading ( Reconnect to [your-folder] ) and a prominent Continue butt...

If multiple people in your lab should share one folder, put the folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud, and follow Shared Lab Accounts . The critical step is making sure the folder is always available offline on every member's laptop.
The two folder cards in the middle : Link Existing Folder on the left, Create New Folder on the right. Either one starts the actual folder-link flow.

BeakerBot in the upper-right : a small sky-blue beaker mascot waving at you. Below the mascot is a white speech bubble that explains the optional walkthrough, plus a button labeled Take the 3-minute walkthrough . Skip past it entirely if you already know what you want to do.
The RISE credentials stamp in the bottom-right : a small badge that names the academic project this app was built under (a UW-Madison RISE grant). Pure authority signal; nothing to click.
Welcome. BeakerBot waves and gives you a two-sentence pitch for ResearchOS. There is also a small heart easter egg if you click the mascot.
Data security. The core promise: your data NEVER leaves your computer. No upload, no central server, no telemetry on your research. See Security for the full story.
Folder choice. Local (recommended for solo) or cloud-synced (for cross-device or multi-person lab). Local skips the next beat and closes the modal; cloud advances to beat 4.

Cloud provider. Picks the cloud you want to host the folder in (OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, Box, iCloud) and links you to the per-provider setup guide. Closing the modal returns you to the picker; the folder link itself still happens through the picker cards above.

Click Link Folder if you already have a ResearchOS folder. The button opens your operating system's folder picker. Alternatively, you can drag a folder directly onto the "Link Existing Folder" card instead of clicking the button. The card highlights with a dashed blue border a...

To create a new folder, type a name in the Folder Name input on the Create New Folder card first. The Choose Location button is disabled until the name field is non-empty. Once you type a name, click Choose Location to open the OS folder picker and select where to save it.
The browser asks for permission to read and write that folder. Click Allow on every visit so you don't get reprompted.

ResearchOS initializes the folder structure (e.g., , ) and shows the user-picker screen. After you pick or create a username, the in-product BeakerBot tour auto-fires on the empty account. That tour is a different thing from the walkthrough modal on this picker page; see Welco...
ResearchOS uses the File System Access API, which is only available in Chromium-based browsers (Chrome, Edge, Brave). Safari and Firefox are not supported. See Browser Requirements for details.

Clicking Take the 3-minute walkthrough opens a 4-beat modal (welcome, data security, folder choice, cloud provider). It runs the BeakerBot intro in slide form, then closes and returns you to this same picker. The modal never picks a folder for you. Returning users skip it enti...
If you've used ResearchOS before, point it at the same folder you used last time. Your projects, tasks, methods, and notes will load exactly as you left them.
Skip the folder picker and visit Demo Mode instead. It opens the app at with a seeded fake yeast lab you can click around in. No folder, no install, edits disappear on reload.
If you clear the site's data (e.g., browser settings → clear data), the folder handle is wiped. Your data on disk is untouched. You just have to pick the folder again next time you open ResearchOS.

The first screen you see on a fresh install. (Screenshot pending recapture: the current layout floats BeakerBot in the upper-right corner with a Take the 3-minute walkthrough CTA, and the RISE credentials stamp sits in the bottom-right. The two cards in the middle still drive ...
The folder-connect screen with two cards: Link Existing Folder on the left and Create New Folder on the right, plus a demo button and setup-guide link below.

entries.title: Creating a User
bodySnippets: Each person in the folder picks a unique username. Their data lives under users/<username>/.
ResearchOS has no central account system. Each "user" is a named folder inside in your data directory, with their own projects, tasks, methods, and notes underneath.

In this model, the folder is the lab . Anyone with access to the same folder can pick a username on the user-picker and become a member, no invite step needed. If a folder is private to you, you're a lab of one. If a folder is shared via OneDrive / Google Drive / Dropbox / iCl...

If you share a laptop, or you're storing the folder in a shared cloud, you can set a per-user password. Go to Settings → Security → Set Password . The password is PBKDF2-hashed and stored in . You can also set or change a password directly from the user-picker by hovering a us...
Click your avatar in the top-right of the header, then Switch user . Pick another user from the list. Your view and data reload to that user's namespace.

One user can be marked as the main user . They get a star badge on their avatar in the picker and are auto-selected as the default on the next visit. To set a main user, hover any user row on the picker and click the star (outline) icon that appears on the right. The main badg...

To rename a user, hover their row on the user-picker and click the pencil (edit) icon. The row switches to an inline input with the current username pre-filled. Edit the name and press Enter or click the green checkmark to save. Press Escape or the red X to cancel. The same us...

To delete a user, hover their row and click the trash icon. A two-step confirmation dialog appears with an optional "Archive data before deletion" checkbox (checked by default). The archive step downloads a ZIP of the user's folder before the directory is removed.

On the user-picker screen, click + Create New User and type a username in the input field. The app accepts letters, numbers, and underscores only (e.g., , ). Hyphens are not accepted and will trigger a validation error.
Click Create & Login .
A directory at is created and you're signed in. The app initializes a full subdirectory tree inside it: , , , , , , , , , , , , and .

Brand-new users land in BeakerBot's welcome tour, a short guided walkthrough on your real account. Plan on roughly five to ten minutes, depending on which features you opt into. See Welcome Tour (BeakerBot) for the full walkthrough, or skip it and explore on your own.

Forgot your password? Delete from your folder (in Finder or Explorer) and the password gate goes away. Your data is untouched. This means a password isn't real security, it's a deterrent on a shared machine.

The user-picker screen, shown right after you connect a folder. (Screenshot pending recapture; the Lab Mode button shown in older captures has been removed and the BeakerBot brand mark added. Use ?wikiCapture=1 when recapturing.)
The user-picker screen with the + Create New User button, the existing-user list, and the BeakerBot brand mark in the gradient pill at the top.

entries.title: Demo Mode
headings: Reading the docs from inside the demo
Three ways to try the app
Every name in the demo is invented
bodySnippets: An in-browser preview of ResearchOS, seeded with a fake yeast lab. No folder picker, nothing to install, edits disappear on reload.

Open (e.g., research-os-xi.vercel.app/demo , the canonical public URL as of this writing; check the project site for the current link if it has moved) and you land straight inside ResearchOS as a researcher named alex , with a populated lab to click around in. Nothing touches ...

The Home page opens with four projects, all prefixed DEMO: so it's obvious nothing here is real research. Each project has experiments on the Gantt, methods in the library, a couple of PCR protocols, a funded purchase list, and a few results pages with lab notes. Other seeded ...

Bare stays at in the URL bar and renders the Home page. Click any nav link (Gantt, Methods, Calendar) and the URL switches to the regular app path ( , ). Demo state follows you through those navigations via a sticky session flag, so the watermarked data and the demo affordance...
Four seeded users are available from the user-picker. Knowing who is who matters because the lab-head and shared-records walkthroughs depend on signing in as the right researcher.

Everything you do in (dragging a Gantt bar, editing a lab note, adding a task, dropping in an image) stays in this browser tab. There's no disk write, no upload, no account. Refresh the tab and the demo resets to its seed state. Good for kicking the tires, not for real research.

Every demo experiment, goal, and event gets its start and end date rebased on the fly so the demo's "today" matches your real today. Visit the demo this week and upcoming experiments stay upcoming, the Gantt scrolls to the current week, and the calendar shows events around now...

Method history, PCR protocol creation dates, and lab note timestamps stay put, because they read as history rather than schedule. The rebase is also idempotent, so visiting twice in the same day doesn't accumulate drift.
Two floating affordances stay with you across every route inside the demo, regardless of whether the URL is a path or the equivalent live app path ( , , and so on).

In the bottom-right corner, a darker pill-shaped Read the docs button (with a small upward-right arrow glyph) shows up whenever the current view has a matching wiki page. Click it on and you land on the Methods wiki entry, ready to read about what you just clicked. The button ...

The mirror move works from the wiki side. Feature pages with a demo-able view embed an amber inline call-out (the component) that drops you into for that view. Each call-site picks its own label, so you'll see variants like Try the Gantt view , Try methods in the demo , Try th...

A floating amber Leave Demo button lives in the bottom-right and follows you across every route while the demo flag is set. It's undismissable, so you never lose the exit. Clicking it opens a dialog titled Leave the demo? with two choices:
alex (member). The default sign-in. Owns all four DEMO: projects and is the user you start as on .
morgan (member). Shares one project with alex and drops comments into alex's threads, so any view that filters by shared records shows real cross-user activity.

Dr. Mira Castellanos (account_type ). The PI of the demo lab. Mira is the user to pick when exploring the lab-head walkthrough, the lab inbox, the PI audit log, the announcements, and the flag-for-review notifications. She owns the orange (#f97316) user color in the comment th...

Dr. Sam Whitley (member, archived). Sam was archived by mira on 2026-03-15 and exists to demo the user-archiving feature. His historical comments and methods are still visible across the lab, but he no longer shows up in assignment pickers.

The amber Leave demo button at the top resets all demo state in this tab and drops you on the connect-folder screen, ready to pick a real folder (or start a new one). Demo edits are not saved to disk anywhere, so they go away with the reload. If you opened from a tab that was ...
The smaller Keep exploring the demo link at the bottom just closes the dialog if you clicked Leave Demo by mistake.
(this page). Fastest. Open the URL, click around, no setup. Edits are temporary and vanish on reload. Best for a first look or for sharing a link with a labmate.

The "Or download as a starter folder" link. On the connect-folder screen there's a small text link under the big Explore demo in browser button. It downloads with the same seeded yeast lab. Unzip it, point ResearchOS at the unzipped folder, and your edits persist on disk for a...

Connect a real folder. For actual research work. See Connecting Your Folder for the picker flow, and Shared Lab Accounts for putting that folder in OneDrive / Drive / Dropbox / iCloud so your whole lab shares it.

The demo lab is full of fake-on-purpose labels: , , , and so on. If you decide to export the demo as a starter folder, rename the projects before you start filing real data in there so nothing reads as a tutorial leftover later.
The Leave Demo dialog. Confirm to reset and return to the folder picker, or keep exploring.
The Leave the demo dialog with a primary Leave demo button and a smaller Keep exploring link below.

entries.title: Exporting from LabArchives
headings: One ZIP can be re-imported later
bodySnippets: ResearchOS imports LabArchives notebooks by reading the Offline Notebook ZIP that LabArchives exports. This page walks you through producing that ZIP.

LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments. ResearchOS doesn't talk to LabArchives in real time, and it doesn't write anything back to your LabArchives account. The hand-off in between is a single file, the Offline Notebook ZIP , that LabArchives genera...

Once you have that ZIP downloaded, the ResearchOS importer reads it directly. You don't unzip it, and you don't need ResearchOS to be signed in to LabArchives. The next page over, Import from LabArchives , covers what the importer does with the file.
You don't need to open the ZIP yourself, but it helps to know what's in there:

Some inline images live in LabArchives' cloud as URLs rather than bundled bytes. The Offline Notebook ZIP keeps the URL but not the image itself for those. ResearchOS handles that on import through a separate Fetch images step, which the import page covers.
With the ZIP downloaded, head to Import from LabArchives to walk through the ResearchOS-side wizard. You'll pick the format, drop the ZIP, preview what's inside, map folders to projects, and apply.
One HTML file per notebook page, holding the page title, every entry on that page, and the entry text.
A subdirectory of attachments for each page, where image uploads and other bundled files sit alongside the HTML.
A top-level manifest that describes the notebook tree, the export timestamp, and who triggered the export.
Sign in to LabArchives at mynotebook.labarchives.com (or your institution's LabArchives URL) in a normal browser tab.

Open the notebook you want to bring into ResearchOS from the notebook switcher in the top-left. Export is one notebook at a time, so if you want several notebooks in ResearchOS, plan to repeat this flow for each.

Open the ≡ Tools menu in the top toolbar and pick Utilities → Create Offline Notebook . (Older builds park this under More → Offline Notebook or Notebook Settings → Export . You're after the same item either way.)
Confirm the request when LabArchives prompts. The request goes into a queue, and LabArchives builds the ZIP server-side. You can close the tab while it works.
Wait for the email. LabArchives mails you a link as soon as the ZIP is ready. Small notebooks take a minute or two, large ones with many attachments can take much longer.

Click the link in the email and download the ZIP to your computer. The file is usually named something like . Save it somewhere you can find later (your Downloads folder is fine), and do not unzip it . ResearchOS reads the archive as a single file.

LabArchives occasionally renames menu items or moves them between toolbars. If a label below doesn't match what you see on screen, look for the same underlying concept: you're asking LabArchives to produce an Offline Notebook export of a single notebook. Other names you might ...

ResearchOS's importer is idempotent against the same ZIP. If you re-run the import on the same file, pages you've already imported are skipped. If you export a fresher ZIP from LabArchives later and run it through, only the new pages land. See Re-running the import for the det...

entries.title: User archiving
bodySnippets: When a lab member leaves (graduates, rotates out, switches projects), archiving their account is the right move. Archiving hides them from active views but keeps every byte of their data on disk and in the lab's history. They drop out of the login picker, member workload, and ...
The two paths are different on purpose:
Archive is reversible. Unarchive a member and they reappear on every surface as if they never left. Delete is permanent (modulo any archive ZIP you chose to download at delete time).

Archiving is a PI soft-write. Open Settings -> Lab Mode tab and the LabRoster section lists every member. Hover any row to reveal an Archive button on the right. The first archive in a fresh session prompts for the PI password (see Edit session and password ); subsequent archi...
The action does six things atomically:

At the top of the LabRoster section is a Show archived toggle. Flip it on and archived members reappear in the table with a muted style and an Unarchive button instead of Archive. One click (still gated by the edit session) restores them on every surface, and the audit row thi...

Archived members are also filterable on the user picker. By default the picker hides them; a small Show archived checkbox at the bottom of the picker reveals them when you need to sign in as a former member to check their work.
The right tool for "this account was a test that should not exist" is delete, not archive. Archive is for real members whose work you want to keep.

Archive hides the user from active surfaces while preserving their on-disk data. Tasks they own, notes they wrote, purchases they logged, all stay where they are. Anyone with read access still sees them. The archived user just stops appearing in the picker, the member workload...

Delete removes the user's folder from disk entirely. Their data goes with them. The delete affordance lives on the user picker (a trash icon on each row, documented in Creating a user ) and is the right tool only when you genuinely want the data gone (a test account, a duplica...
Sets on the member's metadata entry.
Hides the member from the user picker by default (see filter control below).
Drops the member from member-workload, lab-activity, and recent-activity widgets on the Lab Overview.
Keeps their records (tasks, notes, purchases) readable to anyone who already had access. No permissions are stripped retroactively.
Keeps their comments and announcements visible in their original places, with an archived badge next to their name.
Writes an row to _pi_audit.json recording the actor, target, and timestamp.
A graduate student finished and is no longer doing lab work, but their results and notes still need to be readable for the next person who picks up the project.
A rotation student wrapped up and you do not want them on the member-workload chart for upcoming planning.
An undergrad worked over the summer and is gone for the school year; archive now, unarchive when they return.

Members never see an "archived" banner on their own account. If a PI archives someone who later signs back in, that person signs in normally and sees their data as they left it; only the cross-lab surfaces stop including them. The audit log is the source of truth for "why am I...
The LabRoster in Settings, Lab Mode tab. Hover a member row to surface the Archive action.
The LabRoster table in Settings with three members listed and an Archive button revealed on hover for one of the rows.

entries.title: Welcome Tour (BeakerBot)
headings: What the welcome tour actually is
Phase 2: walkthrough on your real account
How feature picks change which tabs you see
bodySnippets: BeakerBot's welcome tour is a guided walkthrough on your real account. It opens with a short setup Q&A, then drops you into a live tour that helps you create your first project, method, and experiment right in the app. Plan on five to fifteen minutes, depending on which option...

When you create a brand-new ResearchOS user, BeakerBot pops up to show you around. The tour runs on your real account, against your real local-first data folder. Anything BeakerBot creates (a sample project, a placeholder method, a first experiment) lands on disk just like nor...
Three things are true about every run:
The tour auto-fires only when three conditions all hold:
Existing users never see the tour automatically. If you signed in before BeakerBot existed, your sidecar migrates quietly to the new schema with no auto-fire, no banner, no nag.
To run the tour after the fact, go to Settings > Onboarding and click Re-run tour . BeakerBot re-mounts in place immediately (no page reload) starting from the welcome screen.

The first few minutes are a short Q&A that BeakerBot uses to shape the rest of the tour and which tabs appear in your sidebar. Every question is a radio pick with a sensible default if you click Skip this step .

The first call is whether you are flying solo (one user, your own account) or running a multi-person lab (everyone points their ResearchOS at the same shared folder). The pick determines whether the PI follow-up question (Q1c, below) fires and whether the Lab Links surface is ...

Conditional on Q1 = Lab. After picking Lab, BeakerBot follows up with a single binary question: are you the PI, or are you a member? Picking PI sets to on this account; picking Member leaves it at the default. One person in the lab fills the PI slot. The picker badge in the lo...
Five questions about ResearchOS surfaces and preferences:
These answers drive both which tabs appear in your sidebar and which conditional walkthrough clusters fire in Phase 2.

The final setup question asks whether you want a tab for saving bookmarks: VPN links, lab calendars, freezer inventory spreadsheets, manuscript drafts, and so on. Each card holds a URL plus a label so you can jump straight to the resource.

The surface name is account-type-conditional: solo accounts see Links ; lab accounts see Lab Links , a shared tab visible to everyone in the lab. Saying yes to Q7 adds the tab and fires the conditional walkthrough later in Phase 2.

This is where the tour starts touching real data. BeakerBot guides you through a universal sequence covering the major surfaces (Home, Project, Workbench, Hybrid Editor, Gantt, Settings, Search, Wiki), followed by four conditional clusters that fire only when the corresponding...

The project page is where every experiment, method, and task you attach to a project comes back together in one view. The Overview box at the top is yours to fill in (hypothesis, motivation, why this project exists); the Results, Methods, and Activity sections below fill thems...

New accounts land on Home with two default widgets pre-pinned: Upcoming tasks and Today's events. This cluster teaches the canvas itself, that each tile is a snapshot that expands into a full popup, and that the layout is per-user.

Two surfaces live in the top bar: the bell (anything that needs your attention: reminders for upcoming work, updates from labmates, mentions on your writeups) and the inbox next to it (files sent in from outside the app, like Telegram photos or shared attachments). The notific...

Methods are your reusable protocol library: write a technique once here, then attach it to every experiment that uses it instead of rewriting steps each time. BeakerBot walks through category creation, then opens two purpose-built builders (PCR and LC Gradient) before showing ...

Methods are the recipe; the Workbench is where you actually run them. Every experiment gets its own entry with space for notes, results, attached protocols, and files. This is the page you spend most of your time on, so BeakerBot folds the page intro directly into the single c...

The hybrid editor cluster walks you through the experiment note editor in thirteen beats. The arc starts with a framing pair (notes-vs-results split, then a one-beat scope note that the editor is the same one used everywhere in the app), then drops into the markdown deep-dive ...
After the hybrid editor cluster, BeakerBot introduces the standalone Notes and Lists panels on the Workbench:
Six universal beats teach core Gantt mechanics. Lab accounts see an additional seven-beat share-feature cluster:
Lab accounts only (gated on Q1 = lab): seven share-feature beats follow the universal arc:
Goals overview (gated on Q4 = yes): a single step fires after the share cluster and explains the Goals overlay on the Gantt toolbar.

Settings is the last stop on the universal arc. BeakerBot opens with a narration beat that establishes scope (everything about the account: appearance, visible tabs, integrations, the AI Helper prompt, and the re-run button), then walks two personalization beats on the Gantt t...
search-demo. BeakerBot opens the Search tab and live-types a query that matches the experiment you created earlier. The highlighted result lands in the list.
The final universal cluster introduces the help icon in the top-right of the AppShell:
Four conditional clusters fire after the wiki-pointer cluster. Each cluster gates on the matching Q answer being yes :
The final step is . BeakerBot says "You're set! Here's to many great experiments ahead." and presents a single Let's go button.
Clicking Let's go triggers the outro:
Clicking I've got it from here at any earlier step skips the intervening steps and jumps directly to .

BeakerBot is the canonical ResearchOS mascot: a sky-blue chemistry beaker with pastel-rainbow liquid, dot eyes, and measurement-mark cheek dashes. The voice is funny and playful throughout. The tour draws from nine or more poses, chosen contextually:

A persistent link in the footer of every step. Click it, confirm in the sub-modal, and BeakerBot jumps to . The run gets recorded as a skip rather than a completion, so re-running from Settings still works.
Each step (after the intro) has a Skip this step link. If a later step depends on the artifact this step creates, BeakerBot silently creates a placeholder version with .
Closing the tab partway through writes the current step plus every artifact created so far into . The next time you open ResearchOS with this folder, a small modal asks:
Clicking Re-run tour in Settings > Onboarding performs an inline that clears , , , , , , and in a single write. The controller then calls to re-mount the tour in place. No page reload.
Your Q1 through Q7 answers determine the visible-tab set through two helpers in :
The rules:

Settings can manually hide a tab that the picks would otherwise show. Settings cannot unhide a tab that the picks excluded. To get a hidden tab back, re-run the tour and flip the matching Q answer, or toggle it on directly in Settings > Onboarding > Visible tabs .

In development builds, a small BeakerBot button sits in the bottom-right floating cluster alongside the data-folder and switch-user controls. Clicking it opens a dropdown with three escape hatches for driving the tour without walking it from scratch. The whole control is gated...
The three actions are:
The tour is one continuous experience inside the app, not a separate demo sandbox. There is no fake fixture you discard on close.

BeakerBot drives the pacing with a Got it, next or Next button on each step. You can also click Skip this step on any individual step, or the persistent I've got it from here link in the footer to jump straight to the goodbye screen.
Closing the tab mid-tour is safe. The next time you open the folder, BeakerBot offers Resume , Restart , or Discard .
The folder has no entry, or the file is empty.
There is no on disk for the active user, or the file has no and no field.
The session is not in a completed or skipped state. The dev flag overrides both completed and skipped states so the tour can be driven against fixture data.
Q2: Will you track lab purchases? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q3: Want calendar feeds? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q4: Want a goal-tracking page? (Yes / No / Maybe later)
Q5: Want a Telegram bot for image inbox? (Yes / No / Maybe later)

Q6: AI Helper prompt size. ResearchOS can paste a system prompt into Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini so the assistant understands ResearchOS terminology. Full is the default. Medium and Minimal trim the prompt for smaller-context models. No or Maybe later skips the AI Helper tour c...
Telegram (Q5 = yes). The step covers the inline pairing flow and a quick demo of sending an image from Telegram and watching it appear in your image inbox.

Purchases (Q2 = yes). An eight-step cluster in two phases. Phase 1 teaches on your empty page: intro, create-button click, form fill, autocomplete demo. Phase 2 warps into a read-only viewer over Alex's demo account to show the analytics surface, then navigates back.
Calendar (Q3 = yes). The step covers the inline calendar-feed subscribe flow. The new feed appears in the Calendar tab when you click Next.
Links / Lab Links (Q7 = yes). The step walks the bookmark tab: adding a card with a URL and label, understanding the difference between personal Links (solo) and shared Lab Links (lab).
A full-screen overlay mounts. BeakerBot cheers and confetti fires (~1.8 s).
BeakerBot shifts to waving pose and translates off-screen (~1.8 s).
The overlay fades out (~0.8 s). The route lands on .
Auto-cleanup runs silently in the background, removing demo artifacts and leaving your first real project intact.

entries.title: Integrations
bodySnippets: Optional add-ons that connect ResearchOS to outside services. All are off by default.

entries.title: Calendar Feeds
headings: Add a calendar by ICS URL
Where to copy the ICS URL from
What URLs the server proxy will fetch
Want to edit linked events from ResearchOS?
Secret URLs leak your whole calendar
bodySnippets: Overlay events from Google, Outlook, iCloud, or any public iCal URL on the ResearchOS calendar. Read-only, so you can plan around them without ResearchOS ever writing back to the source.

Each linked calendar shows its events on the Calendar page in the color you pick, sitting alongside ResearchOS tasks and events. Clicking an event opens a popup with the title, time, and any location or notes the source calendar published.

Linked calendars are read-only. ResearchOS fetches a public iCal (ICS) URL on a timer and overlays the events. It never writes back to Google, Outlook, or Apple. To edit an event, open it in its source app, and the change syncs into ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh.
Go to the Calendar page. The Linked Calendars button sits top-right, next to + New Event . A small blue badge on the button counts how many subscriptions are currently turned on.
Inside the panel, the Where do I find this URL? link expands a per-provider checklist that matches whichever Provider you have selected. The summaries below are the same steps in long form.
Pick Other (any public iCal URL) in the Provider dropdown. That covers university course schedules, lab seminar series, conference timetables, sports schedules, anything that ends in or starts with .
Each subscription becomes a row in the Connected list at the top of the panel. The row shows:

ResearchOS refetches each feed at most once every 15 minutes per browser session. The fetch goes through a small server proxy (the same Vercel function that bypasses CORS for Google, Outlook, and iCloud), and the proxy caches the response on its own edge cache for another 15 m...
If you just added an event in the source calendar and want it on the ResearchOS grid right now, hard-refresh the browser tab or wait out the 15-minute window.

The proxy is the small server endpoint that fetches each ICS URL on your behalf, because browsers can't fetch directly (Google doesn't set the right CORS headers). The proxy refuses a few classes of URL outright so it can't be used to scan internal infrastructure:
If a paste fails with one of those errors, double-check that the URL is the public ICS variant of the calendar (not the regular web-app share link).

Repeating events (e.g., a weekly seminar) are expanded into one chip per occurrence inside a ±2-year window around today. That keeps a 20-year-long daily recurrence from spawning ~7,300 chips on the grid. If you scroll the calendar more than two years backward or forward, repe...

Linked calendars are read-only by design. To make changes, open the event in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar). Your edit shows up in ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh. Earlier ResearchOS builds had an OAuth-backed two-way sync for Google and Out...
Open in a desktop browser.
Click the gear -> Settings -> Settings for my calendars .
Pick the calendar you want to share in the sidebar.
Copy Secret address in iCal format for a private calendar, or Public address in iCal format if the calendar is already public.
Open Outlook on the web -> Settings -> Calendar -> Shared calendars .
Under Publish a calendar , pick the calendar, choose Can view all details , and click Publish .
Copy the ICS link Outlook generates.
Open the Calendar app on macOS, or sign in to in a browser.
Right-click the calendar in the sidebar -> Share Calendar -> Public Calendar .
Copy the share link. It starts with , which is fine to paste as-is.
The label you gave it, plus a small uppercase tag with the provider type.
The full ICS URL, truncated to one line.
A Last synced timestamp once ResearchOS has fetched the feed at least once.
A 5-swatch color strip on the left edge of the row. Click any swatch to recolor that calendar's events on the grid.
An On / Off checkbox to hide a calendar's events without removing the subscription.
A red Remove link to drop the subscription. A confirmation dialog reminds you that ResearchOS events aren't touched.
Anything that isn't (so no , no , no ). Plain is rejected outright for security. The prefix is rewritten to first, so iCloud URLs still work.
and the loopback range .
Private IPv4 ranges: , , .
Link-local and the IPv6 equivalents ( , , ).
Responses that don't start with . This catches the case where a calendar share URL redirects to an HTML sign-in page instead of returning iCal text.
Click Linked Calendars , then scroll the panel down to Or paste a public iCal / ICS URL .
Pick the source from the Provider dropdown (iCloud / Apple Calendar, Google Calendar, Outlook / Office 365, or Other (any public iCal URL)).
Type a Label (this is what shows up on the Calendar page) and click one of the 10 swatches under Color .
Paste the URL into the ICS URL field and click Add Calendar . The button shows Testing... while ResearchOS fetches the feed to confirm it parses.
If the feed parses but contains no events within a ±2-year window of today, you'll get a confirmation dialog before the subscription saves. That's the normal case for stale or empty calendars.
Paste a URL exactly as iCloud (or any other client) gives it to you. The server proxy rewrites it to automatically before fetching.

Google's Secret address in iCal format grants read access to every event title, time, and note in that calendar to anyone who has the URL. Treat it like a password (e.g., don't paste it into Slack, a public repo, or a shared notes doc). ResearchOS keeps the URL in your private...

The edge cache also serves the previous response while fetching a fresh copy in the background (stale-while-revalidate, up to 1 hour). In the worst case an event you just added could take up to 75 minutes to appear (15 min client window + 60 min stale window). Hard-refreshing ...
The Linked Calendars panel with a connected ICS subscription and the paste-a-URL form.

entries.title: LabArchives
headings: Exporting an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives
Path 2: Manual folder or ZIP drop
Skip image fetch on the first pass
bodySnippets: Bring notebooks out of LabArchives and into ResearchOS as native, file-on-disk tasks — and recover the inline images that LabArchives keeps in the cloud, all without any API credentials.

LabArchives and ResearchOS sit at opposite ends of a hand-off, not in a live sync. LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments; ResearchOS is where you bring those notebooks once you want them on disk in a structured form. There's no two-way connection, ...
Everything runs through one entry point under Settings -> LabArchives :

LabArchives stores inline images in two ways internally. The legacy path, called Form-A , embeds the image as a binary file inside the notebook page entry itself. The newer path, Form-B , uploads the image to LabArchives' cloud and leaves only a URL behind in the entry body.

When you generate an Offline Notebook export ZIP, only the Form-A images come along. The Form-B URLs are still in the entry text, but the image bytes were never bundled. In a typical recent notebook that's roughly half the inline images.

If you import the ZIP without fetching Form-B images, the wizard writes a placeholder for each one. Your notes still come across; the images at those URLs simply aren't there yet. You can recover them at any time using one of the three paths described below, or remove individu...
When images are recovered, inline images land in the task's folder and the markdown is rewritten to point at the local copy. File attachments (non-image uploads) land in .
The ZIP that ResearchOS imports is LabArchives' built-in Offline Notebook export. Both the import and any image-recovery run depend on having this file first.
Three paths exist to pull Form-B images into your notes. All three are credential-free: ResearchOS never asks for your LabArchives password.

The Generate browser script tab (in the wizard's Fetch images step, or in the per-task Rehydrate panel) shows a self-contained JavaScript snippet you paste into the browser console while you're logged into LabArchives. The script:
Drop the downloaded ZIP into the Drop your own images tab (or drag it straight into the panel) and ResearchOS matches filenames automatically.

If you already have the Form-B image files on disk (from a previous download, a screenshot batch, or an export from another tool), use the Drop your own images tab. Drop a folder or a containing the image files and ResearchOS matches them to the expected filenames automaticall...

This path also accepts the ZIP produced by the DevTools script above, which is why the two-tab flow is designed the way it is: generate and download in the DevTools tab, then drop the result in the manual tab.
After import, any task that still has unresolved Form-B images shows broken image placeholders in the Lab Notes editor. Clicking a broken placeholder opens a small popup with three options:

Import from LabArchives — the import wizard. You download an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives and feed it to the wizard. Every notebook page becomes a ResearchOS task; every folder becomes a project you can map to your existing project list. No credentials, works offline.
Generate browser script — the DevTools-script path (see below).
Drop your own images — the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below).
Checks that it's running on — it refuses to do anything on other origins.
Fetches each missing image URL using your existing session cookies (no password, no API key — just the session the browser already has from your login).
Packages all successful fetches into a single ZIP and triggers one browser download.

Find on LabArchives — opens the original image URL in a new tab pointed at . If you're logged in, the image loads and you can right-click -> Save. Then use Replace from disk below, or drag the saved file onto the note.

Replace from disk — opens a file picker. Pick a local image file; ResearchOS writes it to , rewrites the markdown reference, and removes the entry from the task's sidecar so the popup doesn't re-appear on the next open.
Remove reference — deletes the broken placeholder from the markdown entirely. Use this when you don't have the image and don't need it.
Open the notebook you want to export in LabArchives on the web.
Click More in the top toolbar -> Offline Notebook . (LabArchives sometimes labels this Export Notebook or Backup Notebook depending on your institution's build.)
Wait. Large notebooks take minutes to assemble; LabArchives emails you when the ZIP is ready.
Download the ZIP from the email link. Don't unzip it — ResearchOS reads the raw archive.
Go to Settings -> LabArchives and click Open import... on the Import from LabArchives card.
Drag the Offline Notebook ZIP into the upload step. The wizard parses the bundle and surfaces a preview of pages, folders, and inline images.
On the Pick format step, leave the default (Offline Notebook). PDF and Chrome-print formats are stubbed for a later version.
On the Preview step, glance through the page list and confirm the entry counts look right.
On the Project mapping step, point each LabArchives folder at one of your existing ResearchOS projects, or let the wizard create a new project named .

If the wizard detected Form-B images (and you're not in demo mode), a Fetch images step appears. It shows two tabs: Generate browser script — the DevTools-script path (see below). Drop your own images — the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below). Either fetch images now or cl...
The Apply step writes everything to disk. When it's finished, the Done step lists what landed where.
In the Fetch images wizard step (or per-task Rehydrate panel), click the Generate browser script tab and copy the script.
Open LabArchives in your browser and navigate to any page of the notebook you imported. You just need to be logged in.
Open DevTools (F12 or right-click -> Inspect), switch to the Console tab, paste the script, and press Enter.
The script logs progress in the console and triggers a single ZIP download when done. Save the ZIP somewhere easy to find.
Switch back to ResearchOS and drop the ZIP into the Drop your own images tab. The panel shows how many filenames matched and stages them for import.
Click Continue with N images . The Apply step writes them to and rewrites the markdown references.

It's fine to skip image recovery during the initial import. Form-B images become placeholders you can clean up later. The broken-image popup in any imported note lets you handle them one at a time, and the per-task "Rehydrate images" panel lets you batch them all at once.

Each notebook page becomes a directory under with a body, a folder for file attachments, a folder for inline images, and an for provenance. Tasks are marked complete, with and set to the page's newest entry timestamp.

The script runs entirely in your browser. ResearchOS never sees the image bytes until you explicitly drop the resulting ZIP back into the wizard. The URLs are hard-coded into the script (no dynamic eval, no network round-trip back to ResearchOS). The flag only attaches cookies...

If you saved an image via "Find on LabArchives" and it filename-matches an outstanding placeholder, dragging it straight onto the note triggers the same recovery pipeline as the file picker — no need to open the popup first.

entries.title: Telegram Bot
headings: When both an experiment and a note are open
Pairing isn't saved until your bot receives a message
During setup, your first photo just goes to the Inbox
Photos render inline, even with spaces in the name
Right-click works on a single row too
bodySnippets: Pair your account with a Telegram bot so phone photos from the bench are routed to the right experiment with a single tap.

Once paired, you send photos to a single Telegram bot from your phone. Every photo (or album) triggers an inline-keyboard prompt in your Telegram chat: the bot asks where the photo should go, you tap a button, and it lands there within a couple of seconds. Nothing routes silen...
After you confirm the destination, the bot asks for a caption. Type one back and it is stored alongside the image. Send to leave the photo without one.

At the top of every page in ResearchOS there's a small pill that either reads "Connect Telegram" (gray, when no bot is paired) or with a green pulsing dot when polling is healthy. The pill turns amber if another tab is already polling the same bot, and red if Telegram has reje...

If the bridge hits a transient hiccup like a token refresh blip, a flaky Wi-Fi connection, or a brief Telegram API timeout, the pill switches to amber with a pulsing dot and adds a small label next to the bot username. The poller backs off and retries on its own, doubling the ...

Each time a new photo arrives over Telegram while this tab is open, the pill grows a green counter on the right (e.g., after three photos have come in). It's a quick confirmation that the bridge is alive and that the photos you just sent from your phone actually made it across...
Every inbound photo triggers the same inline-keyboard routing flow, regardless of what is open in ResearchOS at the time. The bot never silently auto-attaches.
The bot's first message reads "Got a photo. Where should it go?" and presents a keyboard layout that depends on which surface, if any, is open in ResearchOS when the photo arrives:

Each experiment in the picker shows up as two lettered rows, one for Lab Notes and one for Results, so the destination is committed in a single tap. When you use the quick-pick from an open experiment, the Lab Notes / Results choice is right there as buttons A and B.

Notes are first-class destinations alongside experiments. When a note popup is open in ResearchOS, the bot offers a one-tap "Attach to this note " shortcut. When nothing is open, the full picker has a separate Notes section listing your most recent notes; tap a lettered row to...

If you have an experiment popup AND a note popup open at the same time, the bot asks first which surface to use: A) the experiment , B) the note , or C) pick another experiment or note . Picking the experiment then runs the usual Lab Notes / Results follow-up prompt; picking t...

If you tap 📥 Inbox in the bot's keyboard, the photo lands at and the Inbox badge in the top bar increments. Click the badge to open the inbox modal. Each row shows a thumbnail, the caption you sent, and when it arrived. From here you can move a photo into the currently-open s...
When you come back to your desk with a stack of phone photos that all belong to the same experiment, the inbox lets you file them in one move instead of clicking Move to active on each row:

With one or more rows selected, right-click any selected row (or click the ⋯ button) to open a context menu. The top two items read Send to task... and Send to note... for a single row, or Send N items to task... / Send N items to note... when multiple are selected. The task p...

Every photo begins with the same opener from the bot: "Got a photo. Where should it go?" followed by the inline keyboard. After you tap a destination (and optionally a sub-tab), the bot saves the file and asks for a caption. Reply with a sentence and it is stored as the image'...

Albums (multiple photos sent together) are bundled into a single routing prompt instead of one prompt per photo. After the destination is confirmed you can choose to name the batch in one go or caption each photo individually.
Send to the bot at any time for a refresher on how it behaves.
Click the Telegram pill again. The modal lists the current pairing with a Disconnect bot button. Clicking it deletes your local and polling stops. Existing photos and notes aren't touched.
The bot itself stays alive on Telegram's side. To delete the bot entirely, send to @BotFather and confirm with the bot's username.

Polling Telegram from two ResearchOS tabs at once would have them fight over the same message cursor. The app holds a cross-tab lock in , so only one tab polls at a time. The other tabs show an amber pill with the label and pick up automatically if the active tab closes.

An experiment is open in ResearchOS. The keyboard shows two quick-pick options for that experiment: Lab Notes (saves into 's image folder) or Results (saves into the image folder), plus a Pick another escape hatch.
A note popup is open. The keyboard offers a one-tap shortcut to attach to that note plus an escape to the full picker.
Both an experiment and a note are open. The bot disambiguates with an A/B/C prompt asking which surface you mean before proceeding.

Nothing is open. The bot shows the full picker: your active and in-progress experiments (each with Lab Notes / Results rows), a Notes section listing your most recent notes, and a 📥 Inbox option at the bottom.
Lab Notes routes the photo into the folder under .
Results routes it into the folder under .
Shift-click a second row to select the contiguous range from your last anchor row to this one.
Cmd-click (or Ctrl-click on Windows / Linux) to toggle individual rows in and out of the selection without disturbing the rest.
Selected rows pick up a blue border and ring. Clicking the empty area of the modal clears the selection.
Open Telegram on your phone or desktop and start a chat with @BotFather .
Send . BotFather first asks for a display name (anything you want), then a username (must end in , e.g., ).
BotFather replies with the bot's API token, a string like . Copy it.
Click the Connect Telegram pill in the top bar of any page in ResearchOS.
Paste the bot token into the Bot token field and click Connect . The modal validates the token with Telegram and shows the bot's username.
On your phone, open a chat with your new bot and send any message (e.g., ). ResearchOS picks up the message, captures your chat id, and the modal flips to a green "Paired" confirmation.
Send any photo to the bot. The bot replies with an inline keyboard asking where to send it. Tap a button and the photo appears in ResearchOS within a couple of seconds.

The "send a message" step is how the bot learns which Telegram chat belongs to you. Until that first message arrives, the modal sits at the "waiting" screen. You can cancel from that screen at any time.

The bot can route to a lot of places, and showing the full destination picker on your very first send (before you have your bearings) is more overwhelming than helpful. So while you are walking through setup, the bot keeps it simple: the first photo you send gets a single Plac...

Whatever the photo ends up named, once it lands in Lab Notes or Results it shows up inline in the markdown rather than as a broken link. Filenames with spaces (a phone document called , say, or a batch name you typed) used to trip up the markdown image reference and quietly dr...

You don't need to select multiple rows first. Right-click any inbox row and pick Send to task... to file that one photo into any experiment, even one you don't have open. It saves opening the experiment's popup just to use the Move to active button.

Anyone with this token can send messages and receive photos as your bot. ResearchOS writes the token to in your data folder and auto-appends a rule so it isn't committed if the data folder is a git repo. Treat the token like a password.
Click the Telegram pill in the top bar to open this pairing modal.
The Telegram pairing modal with the bot-token field, opened from the Telegram pill in the top bar.
The Inbox modal, opened from the Inbox badge in the top bar.
The Inbox modal listing photos sent via Telegram with their captions, timestamps, and Move to active / Delete buttons.

entries.title: Security
headings: What briefly touches a server we operate
What we collect, and what we don't
Two recovery surfaces for your Telegram bot token
Public hosting is opt-in, and the proxies are open
bodySnippets: Your research data stays on your computer. We never see it. This page is the plain-English version of what that means in practice, where the narrow exceptions are, and how you can verify the claim for yourself.

ResearchOS is built as a browser-only app that reads and writes a folder you pick on your own machine. Your notes, results, project and task data, images, attachments, Telegram inbox, and calendar subscriptions all stay in that folder. Nothing about your work is uploaded to a ...

When you pick a data folder, ResearchOS reads and writes everything directly to that folder on your machine. The bytes never leave it. You can open the folder at any time in your operating system's file browser and see exactly what is there.
The folder holds:

Three routes on the ResearchOS server are involved. The first two are CORS-bypass streams that exist because browsers refuse to talk directly to the upstream services. The third is an anonymous page-view ping. None of them ever sees the contents of your data folder.

The two CORS-bypass proxy routes use the most defensive shape we know how to write: HTTPS only, private-IP blocking, redirect re-validation, byte cap, timeout, content-type denylist, and per-IP rate limiting. The Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights endpoints are Vercel-owned s...

The on-disk sidecar can disappear for ordinary reasons: a misshared OneDrive deletion, an iCloud sync hiccup, a lab-mate tidying up. ResearchOS keeps two optional recovery paths so you don't have to start over from BotFather when that happens. Both live on your own machine. Ne...

The first recovery path is a browser-scoped IndexedDB store ( ) that holds a minimal credential row so the app can offer a one-click recovery prompt instead of making you re-pair. The cache stores only , , and , keyed by .

The cache is symmetric in risk with the disk sidecar: both are DevTools-readable on the local machine, and the cache is NOT exposed via cloud-folder share (browser-scoped, not file-scoped). This is actually a small win over disk-only storage for the multi-user-folder case, Ali...

The only network call adjacent to the cache is a round-trip to to confirm the cached token still works before the recovery prompt offers it. That call goes direct from your browser to Telegram, the same path the bot polling already uses. The Forget button in Settings -> Data i...

The second recovery path is an encrypted backup file on disk, off by default. If you turn it on, either via the checkbox in the Telegram pairing dialog or via Settings -> Notifications & behavior -> Auto-reconnect Telegram bot , ResearchOS writes an encrypted file at next to y...

Lose your account password and you lose the backup. There is no recovery key, no escape hatch, and no copy of your password anywhere in ResearchOS (we never had it). Anyone who knows your account password can also decrypt this backup, so treat the encrypted backup and your acc...

Changing your account password through the normal change- password flow handles the re-encryption automatically. The submit button label flips to "Re-encrypting Telegram backup..." while it runs, and the success banner reads "Password updated. Telegram backup re-encrypted." Th...
The encryption parameters (KDF, cipher, IV / salt sizes, fail-closed decryption semantics) live in SECURITY_AUDIT.md sections 1.5 and 1.6 for readers who want the bytes.

We collect anonymous page-view pings via Vercel Web Analytics and anonymous Core Web Vitals (load and responsiveness timings) via Vercel Speed Insights. When you navigate between pages, anonymous beacons go to Vercel. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodi...

We do not collect anything else. No Sentry, no Google Analytics, no Mixpanel, no PostHog, no Hotjar, no Datadog, no Amplitude. No background phone-home. No crash reporter. No content telemetry. Running against the repo will confirm only and are present, and the network tab wil...

The Report an issue button does not auto-submit anything. When you click it, your browser opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. You see the body, edit it, and click Submit . Nothing happens until you do.
The above claims are real. They have implications you should make peace with before you trust ResearchOS with anything sensitive.
You don't have to take any of this on faith. There are two ways to confirm what the app is doing. The first is built into Settings and takes about thirty seconds.
Your browser already shows every network request the app makes. This is the audit-grade path for anyone who wants to see the bytes themselves.

ResearchOS went through an internal security audit on 2026-05-15. It closed one Critical finding (cross-site scripting via unsanitized markdown HTML across all 8 markdown rendering sites in the app) and several Important findings around the proxy routes, the LabArchives creden...
If you find something that looks wrong, open a GitHub issue (the Report an issue button is the fastest path) or email the maintainer directly. We'd much rather hear about a problem early.
Every experiment, lab note, and result you write.
Every image, PDF, and arbitrary attachment you drop into a note.
The JSON for projects, tasks, dependencies, methods, and PCR protocols.
Your Telegram bot token and any photos that arrived through it.
Your calendar subscription URLs and the events they pulled in.
The optional PBKDF2-hashed password protecting your per-user profile.

Calendar feed sync. When you subscribe to an iCal URL (Google, Outlook, iCloud, a university calendar), the browser asks to fetch the iCal text from the upstream and stream it back. The subscription URL travels in the request header rather than in the URL query string, so it i...

Telegram file CDN. When a photo arrives through your bot, the browser asks to fetch the bytes from Telegram (whose CDN refuses to set CORS headers, so the browser cannot reach it directly). The bot token travels in a request header, never in the URL. The bytes stream straight ...

Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, your browser sends an anonymous page-view beacon to Vercel telling them which route you visited. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies, no project names. Vercel sees your IP address (which they hash...
The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English.
The Offline mode toggle disables the two proxy routes plus the Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights script tags in one click, for anyone who wants zero outbound network from the app surface.
"Where is this stored?" hints on each integration field so you always know which file holds the credential you just entered.
The first, , holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you picked.
The second, , holds three small session-routing strings: the folder name plus its grant timestamp, the currently signed-in user, and (if you are a PI signed in) the primary account.
The third, , exists only if you have paired a Telegram bot. It holds one row per paired-user-in-this-folder, with the minimal credentials , , , keyed by .
Open ResearchOS in Chrome, Edge, or Brave, then open DevTools. F12 works on Windows and Linux. Cmd + Option + I works on macOS.
Switch to the Network tab and check Preserve log so refreshes don't clear the view.
Reload the page. Visit Calendar, Telegram inbox, the experiments you care about. Watch every outbound request as you go.

You should see requests to your own ResearchOS origin (for JavaScript, CSS, and static assets), occasional requests to when a subscribed feed refreshes, occasional requests to when an inbox photo loads, direct requests to when you exchange messages with your bot, and occasiona...

For a second pass, switch from the Network tab to Application -> IndexedDB . ResearchOS uses three IndexedDB databases. The first, , holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you pick...

Open your data folder in Finder or Explorer. Every experiment, note, image, and attachment you have written is sitting there as a regular file you can read. ResearchOS writes to that folder and nowhere else. Quit the app and your data stays exactly where it is.

The first recovery path is a browser-scoped IndexedDB store ( ) that holds a minimal credential row so the app can offer a one-click recovery prompt instead of making you re-pair. The cache stores only , , and , keyed by . The cache is symmetric in risk with the disk sidecar: ...

The second recovery path is an encrypted backup file on disk, off by default. If you turn it on, either via the checkbox in the Telegram pairing dialog or via Settings -> Notifications & behavior -> Auto-reconnect Telegram bot , ResearchOS writes an encrypted file at next to y...

If you share your data folder with a lab-mate over OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive, or iCloud, every byte in it is theirs to read. The per-user password gate stops accidental account-switching inside the app, not someone opening the folder in their own file browser.

We hash account passwords with PBKDF2-SHA-256 (600,000 iterations) so a snooping co-located user can't trivially read them, but the data itself sits in plaintext on disk. Anyone with disk access has it. Turn on OS-level full-disk encryption (FileVault on macOS, BitLocker on Wi...

Your Telegram bot token lives in plaintext in . We auto-gitignore the file so it doesn't slip into a , but anyone who reads the file can post and read messages on your bot's chats. Treat it like any other API key. The optional encrypted backup at (described above) is the one p...

If you self-host ResearchOS on a public Vercel deploy with no auth gate, anyone on the internet can hit the two proxy routes (subject to rate limiting). This is not a data-exfiltration risk because the proxies have no access to your data folder, but it does mean someone could ...

ResearchOS ships three affordances inside Settings: The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English. The Offline mode toggle disables the t...
The Report an Issue modal. You see the body, you edit the body, you choose whether to submit.

The Report an Issue modal, showing the Type radio group, an editable Title field, an editable description, optional auto-attached error details, and the Cancel / Copy Link / Create GitHub Issue buttons at the bottom.

entries.title: Shared Lab Accounts
headings: Setup steps (every member runs these once)
Want to feel out the shared model first?
